chapters.description: The talk turns to body sensation, emotion, and language as rapid responses to threat and uncertainty, with thinking framed as a coping mechanism.

chapters.title: The safety of thinking
chapters.description: He examines why narration and conceptualization can feel protective, even when they are painful, and how this habitual problem-solving runs against practice.

chapters.title: Letting practice work
chapters.description: Brensilver distinguishes willfulness from willingness and describes the kind of effort needed as silence and self-concern become more visible.

chapters.title: Healing and mortality
chapters.description: He speaks about reducing the charge of past wounds, working with fear directly, and acclimating the heart to finitude and death.

chapters.title: Courage and faith
chapters.description: The talk draws on the Buddha, Aquinas, Dogen, and others to frame courage, surrender, and trust as supports for deeper stillness.

chapters.title: Samadhi and clarity
chapters.description: He closes by describing samadhi as the resolution of ambivalence, a state where the mind is no longer split and clarity can show what is here.

title: Guided Metta Meditation - Metta for All Beings (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames metta as one of the Brahma-viharas, or “four species of love,” then shows how loving-kindness can function as cultivation, concentration, and purification. The talk moves from the way metta loosens self-view to a guided practice that begins with the body and phrases, then opens outward to all beings. It ends by holding the sincerity of the wish alongside the fact that suffering is vast and unresolved.
short_summary: A guided metta practice that explores loving-kindness as wisdom, concentration, purification, and a way of releasing self-view.

chapters.title: Four kinds of love
chapters.description: Matthew opens by framing the Brahma-viharas as different ways love meets goodness, suffering, success, and helplessness.

chapters.title: Love changes self-view
chapters.description: He uses a retreat story to show how even a brief moment of metta can reorganize the relationship to self and loosen the self-as-project mode.

chapters.title: Measureless and quiet
chapters.description: The talk turns to the immeasurables and the reminder that loving-kindness may be subtle, quiet, and not defined by intensity.

chapters.title: Cultivation and purification
chapters.description: Matthew explains metta as cultivation, concentration, and purification, including the way practice can bring buried grief or rage to the surface.

chapters.title: Settling into practice
chapters.description: The guided section begins with body, posture, and relaxation, then points to worthiness beyond self-judgment and the habit of proving oneself.

chapters.title: Phrases in the present
chapters.description: He introduces the idea of a mind made vast by love and uses simple phrases as a present-moment way to access the heart.

chapters.title: Resting in kindness
chapters.description: The practice widens through the recognition that whatever is arising can be held, whether by quiet rest or by bringing care to what has not yet been loved.

chapters.title: Love in hard places
chapters.description: Matthew names this as non-self-indulgent love and notes how tight spots can reveal the necessity of love that makes hatred less tenable.

chapters.title: All beings at ease
chapters.description: The meditation culminates in the wish for all beings to be safe, protected, and at peace, even while acknowledging that suffering does not end.

title: Guided Meditation: Non-referentiality
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver begins with a light, self-aware basketball anecdote and then turns quickly into a guided meditation on settling, relaxing, and sensing the body as light and gravity. The main teaching explores how the mind locates itself through reference points—body, preference, craving, aversion—and invites a period of practice “without referentiality.” The closing section softens into endings, dissolution, and a Rumi line about living in the nowhere you come from, returning to spaciousness without fixed self-reference.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing into the body and resting without reference points.

chapters.title: Opening and arrival
chapters.description: Matthew opens with a brief basketball story and then invites everyone to settle in for the meditation.

chapters.title: Brightness and body
chapters.description: The practice begins by relaxing everything except brightness, filling the body with light, and letting the center of gravity drop into the belly.

chapters.title: Held by earth
chapters.description: He emphasizes gravity, the earth holding us fast, and fuller breaths through the belly.

chapters.title: Softening more
chapters.description: The instruction becomes simple and repetitive, encouraging a gradual softening into the sitting.

chapters.title: Reference points
chapters.description: Matthew uses the image of a phone pinging towers to explain how we locate ourselves through body, preferences, plans, craving, and aversion.

chapters.title: Without referentiality
chapters.description: The meditation shifts into a long refrain asking what it means to be here without reference points and to lay down sight, sound, sensation, and thought.

chapters.title: Endings and dissolution
chapters.description: Near the end, he invites tolerance for endings, things dissolving, and not needing to pick anything else up.

chapters.title: Nowhere you come from
chapters.description: A Rumi quotation deepens the final spaciousness, and the closing returns again and again to non-identification, including the meditator and the act of teaching itself as just another ping.

title: Dharmette: 'A thing that masquerades as freedom of attention is on the rampage'
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on attention as something deeply tied to love, desire, and ethical life, while arguing that modern technological frenzy distorts all three. He quotes a 2019 Friends of Attention manifesto, then moves through Rilke, Naomi Shihab Nye, and Simone Weil to show how slowing down and staying with experience can loosen craving and hatred. The talk settles into a quiet claim: wise attention is not a technique for control, but a form of prayer that lets the soul be drawn toward the good.
short_summary: A short Dharma talk on how attention, love, and desire are shaped by technology, and how patient attention can become prayer.

chapters.title: Attention and love
chapters.description: Matthew begins by linking fragmented attention with compromised love and the effects of technological frenzy.

chapters.title: The manifesto's warning
chapters.description: He introduces the Friends of Attention manifesto and reads its critique of narrowed desire and compulsive satisfaction.

chapters.title: True freedom of attention
chapters.description: He contrasts false freedom with the burden and dignity of real attention, describing attention as a basic currency of practice.

chapters.title: Craving tightened
chapters.description: He turns to the second noble truth, suggesting that the craving-acquisition-satisfaction-heartbreak loop has become especially tight in modern life.

chapters.title: Rilke and slowing down
chapters.description: Rilke and Naomi Shihab Nye help him name helplessness, restraint, and the need to live more slowly and locally.

chapters.title: Depth over surface
chapters.description: He returns to the relation between attention and desire, showing how patient attention can cultivate fidelity and a deeper kind of engagement.

chapters.title: Attention as prayer
chapters.description: Simone Weil's words frame attention as prayer and as the means by which faults are healed without force.

chapters.title: Giving up hate
chapters.description: He closes by saying deep attention makes hatred harder to sustain and leaves us with a quiet invitation to consider this practice.

title: Somebody, Nobody, Self-Love, Anatta
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on an old Jack Engler teaching about having to be “somebody before being nobody,” then uses that frame to clarify how insight practice depends on some stability rather than collapse into confusion. From there he turns to the language of self-love and anatta, arguing that self-love is better understood as forgiveness, non-clinging, and a first loosening of identification. The talk closes by linking this gentleness to a deeper metacognitive shift in which what once felt like “the place from which I see” becomes something seen.
short_summary: A quiet talk on how self-love and not-self can be understood as stages of loosening identification, not as opposites.

chapters.title: Looking Back
chapters.description: He opens by reflecting on old notes from his earliest Dharma talks and the humility that comes from seeing one’s own earlier confusion and continuity.

chapters.title: Somebody Before Nobody
chapters.description: Matthew revisits Jack Engler’s line about needing to be somebody before being nobody, and uses it to suggest that deconstruction in practice needs a base of psychological strength.

chapters.title: Language and Self
chapters.description: He brings in Wittgenstein to show how the word self can mislead, especially when the same word is used in very different Dharma and psychological contexts.

chapters.title: Self-Love as Forgiveness
chapters.description: Matthew distinguishes self-love from self-affirmation, describing it as forgiveness, innocence, and non-clinging rather than a celebration of a fixed identity.

chapters.title: Not-Self Is Flexibility
chapters.description: He clarifies that not-self is not confusion or diffusion, but a flexible relationship to traits and life events that loosens attachment and aversion.

chapters.title: The Self Model Opens
chapters.description: Using a philosophical reference to Letheby, he describes anatta as a deeper unbinding in which self-related contents become visible as objects in awareness.

chapters.title: Return to Innocence
chapters.description: He ends by circling back to the practical arc: conditioning can be seen clearly, growth can be wished for without clinging, and the self can be met with more innocence each time it reappears.

title: Guided Medtiation: Checking Hindrances, Checking Defilements
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with settling into the body and then scans the five hindrances as a practical pulse check: restlessness, dullness, craving, aversion, and doubt. The emphasis stays gentle and observational, with repeated invitations to notice what is present without forcing anything away. As the practice unfolds, the talk turns toward relaxing the effort to control experience and allowing attention to become more open, unmixed, and receptive.
short_summary: A gentle guided meditation that checks the five hindrances and invites a quieter, less grasping kind of attention.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation opens by landing in the body and establishing a steady, receptive ground for practice.

chapters.title: Scanning Hindrances
chapters.description: The teacher introduces the five hindrances and invites a simple check-in with whatever is present.

chapters.title: Bearing Unpleasantness
chapters.description: The focus turns to how unpleasantness is met in the nervous system and how awareness can hold it without judgment.

chapters.title: Choosing an Anchor
chapters.description: Different possible anchors are named, from breath and body to sound and open awareness.

chapters.title: Unmixed Attention
chapters.description: The guidance emphasizes relaxing, receiving experience, and letting attention bow rather than grasp.

chapters.title: Letting Go Of Control
chapters.description: The talk points toward surrendering control and noticing that goodness does not need to be manufactured.

chapters.title: Putting Down Yardsticks
chapters.description: The closing movement gestures toward releasing comparison and measurement, and simply resting with what is here.

title: Guided Meditation: Softening the Self
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation on softening the felt sense of self through relaxation, safety, and widening awareness. The practice begins with posture and settling, then turns toward the strain of clinging and willfulness, suggesting that letting go reveals a quieter brightness already present. In the later stages, he explores the self as a knot of tension and invites a more porous, fluid way of being, with an emphasis on anicca and the way identity can loosen when the mind feels safe.
short_summary: A guided practice of relaxing effort, meeting clinging gently, and allowing the sense of self to soften and open.

chapters.title: Arrive and Settle
chapters.description: The meditation opens by welcoming listeners, acknowledging the day's condition, and inviting a settled posture.

chapters.title: Relax into Brightness
chapters.description: He guides a full-body softening while asking the listener to stay tethered to the mind's natural brightness and luminosity.

chapters.title: Held by Love
chapters.description: The practice widens into a sense of shared presence, with repeated reminders to relax even the urge to do more.

chapters.title: Clinging and Letting Go
chapters.description: He names the strain of clinging, striving, and willfulness, then asks what remains when those habits are relaxed.

chapters.title: The Knot of Self
chapters.description: The talk shifts to the self as a knot of tension and explores how safety reduces trance and grasping.

chapters.title: Softening and Widening
chapters.description: Using the image of a snagging hangnail, he repeatedly invites softening and widening until attention feels more spacious.

chapters.title: Impermanence and Porosity
chapters.description: He brings in anicca and asks how deeply impermanence runs, pointing to a self that can melt, become porous, and fluid.

chapters.title: Closing Reflection
chapters.description: The meditation ends with one more invitation to notice how the self may be grasped or may open into something larger.

title: Dharmette: Pleasant and Unpleasant Dissolution
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores why dissolution can feel peaceful in one setting and terrifying in another, using emptiness, derealization, and death anxiety as a comparison. He brings in Nagarjuna, a recent study by Aviva Berkovich-Ohana and Stephen Fulder, and Stephen Levine to show how the Dharma changes the meaning of self-loss. The talk closes by returning to impermanence, grief, and the courage that practice can build around mortality.
short_summary: A quiet inquiry into why dissolution can be nourishing or frightening, and how impermanence reshapes that experience.

chapters.title: A peaceful dissolution
chapters.description: Matthew begins with a personal memory of self-boundaries melting away in retreat and how the experience felt exquisitely peaceful.

chapters.title: When emptiness unsettles
chapters.description: He contrasts contemplative emptiness with derealization and depersonalization, noting the overlap in language but not in meaning.

chapters.title: Nagarjuna on emptiness
chapters.description: A passage attributed to Nagarjuna is used to show emptiness as a way of reducing grasping, conflict, and affliction rather than creating numbness.

chapters.title: Why experiences diverge
chapters.description: Matthew cites recent research on dissolution experiences and turns to predictive models of self and world to explain why some states feel nourishing while others feel destabilizing.

chapters.title: Self as fear's effect
chapters.description: He argues that the sense of self is conditioned by fear, especially the deeper fear of death, which makes mortality feel like a threat to the center of subjectivity.

chapters.title: Craving to become
chapters.description: Using Stephen Levine and the Buddha's teaching on bhava tanha, he describes how dissolution can feel like an attack when the self is being protected against non-being.

chapters.title: Impermanence at the center
chapters.description: Matthew returns to practice, suggesting that placing anicca at the center of understanding softens the fear response and changes the tone of letting go.

chapters.title: Grief and courage
chapters.description: He closes with Levine's line that death is safe, then qualifies it with his own view that grief remains irreducible while the Dharma still strengthens courage.

title: Know Thyself, Release Thyself, Merging
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores how meditation can become a quiet process of self-familiarization: seeing one’s tendencies clearly, loosening stale self-stories, and learning what is actually wanted beneath culture and habit. He moves from personality types and shame to embodiment, intimacy, and the way a less defended sense of self can make room for attunement, spontaneity, and even a kind of merging that does not erase boundaries. Several brief references to Winnicott, Thich Nhat Hanh, Octavio Paz, and others help him frame the talk around aliveness, understanding, and love that reveals freedom.
short_summary: A talk on self-knowledge, shame, embodiment, and how loosening identification can deepen intimacy without clinging.

chapters.title: Know Thyself
chapters.description: Brensilver opens by treating self-knowledge as central to both Dharma practice and relationship, and by distinguishing stable tendencies from borrowed cultural wants.

chapters.title: Habit Energies
chapters.description: He sketches greed, aversion, and delusion as basic behavioral patterns and describes learning the landscape of one’s goodness and trouble spots.

chapters.title: Seeing and Being Seen
chapters.description: The talk turns toward intimacy, with an emphasis on sensing yes and no in the body and on the need to be both able and willing to be seen.

chapters.title: New Ways of Thinking
chapters.description: Brensilver argues that meditation suspends habitual meaning-making so thought can become less automatic, less ruminative, and more open to insight.

chapters.title: Disidentifying From Self
chapters.description: He develops the theme of not being alienated from experience while also not being identified with a rigid self-story, especially where shame and pain have hardened the self.

chapters.title: Embodiment and Aliveness
chapters.description: Using Winnicott through Maggie Nelson, he describes feeling real as embodied aliveness that supports spontaneous gesture, resonance, and empathic contact.

chapters.title: Love Without Possession
chapters.description: Thich Nhat Hanh and Octavio Paz are used to show that love must be infused with understanding and must reveal the other person’s freedom rather than possess them.

chapters.title: Merging and Letting Go
chapters.description: He explores flexible boundaries, the safety that allows closeness, and the grief that comes when the self-model catches up with impermanence.

title: Guided Meditation: Arousal and Valence Signifying Nothing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation on working directly with arousal and valence: how intensity, pleasantness, and unpleasantness are sensed in the body, and how they can be held without turning immediately into stories. The practice emphasizes settling with breath, widening the field of experience, and allowing feeling to remain vivid without making it mean too much. As the talk develops, the instruction turns toward decoupling emotion from elaboration so that experience can be present without collapsing into identity or self-judgment.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting bodily feeling with clarity, equanimity, and less identification.

chapters.title: Settling and Soothe
chapters.description: The meditation opens by anchoring to something settled and noticing that intensity can coexist with something soothing in the field of experience.

chapters.title: Pleasant and Unpleasant
chapters.description: He names the difference between subtle pleasant feeling and the stickier pull of pain, worry, and heartache.

chapters.title: Why Feeling Feels Safe
chapters.description: The instructions explore how feeling can seem protective and how the mind treats certain sensations as a basis for safety.

chapters.title: Tracking Arousal
chapters.description: Brensilver defines arousal and valence as body-level phenomena and links them to the felt texture of emotion and craving.

chapters.title: Giving Feeling Space
chapters.description: The practice widens into a larger field where feeling is allowed as much space as it needs, instead of being crowded into a narrow story.

chapters.title: Worth and Unconscious Feeling
chapters.description: He points to how questions of worthiness and value can arise from unconscious feeling and then repeatedly returns to clarity, equanimity, and alertness.

chapters.title: Tranquility Pulls In
chapters.description: A quieter phase develops as tranquility becomes more available and the body is guided to register feeling without forcing interpretation.

chapters.title: Decoupling Thought Loops
chapters.description: The core instruction emerges: when breathing is disturbed, practice separating arousal and valence from automatic loops of thought and letting feeling signify nothing.

chapters.title: Resting in Breath
chapters.description: The meditation settles into resting with whatever is pleasant in the breath, then continuing to stay intimate with breathing as the sense of self softens.

title: Dukkha as Ambivalence in Relational Life
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores dukkha through the lens of ambivalence in love, friendship, family, and group life. He moves from comic and psychoanalytic references to a practical Buddhist account of equanimity: how to stay with mixed feeling, resist the urge to control, and distinguish care from clinging. The talk closes by returning to compassion as love in the face of helplessness, and to the difficulty of living with the imperfection of others without hardening or taking over.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on dukkha as ambivalence in relationships, and equanimity as the capacity to stay with love, helplessness, and imperfection.

chapters.title: A Comic Opening
chapters.description: A satirical newspaper headline about a 'better soulmate' introduces the unstable wish for others to complete us.

chapters.title: Love and Lack
chapters.description: Lacan and the First Noble Truth frame intimacy as a place where projection, disappointment, and real beauty all meet.

chapters.title: Pain and Disconnection
chapters.description: The Buddha’s back pain and a bleak radio story point to the unavoidable suffering built into relationship and group life.

chapters.title: What Ambivalence Means
chapters.description: Matthew defines dukkha as ambivalence and uses examples from emotion and mood to show how mixed feeling is ordinary.

chapters.title: Freud and Mixed Feeling
chapters.description: Drawing on Adam Phillips and Freud, he argues that love and hate, trust and suspicion, self-criticism and self-love are intertwined.

chapters.title: Equanimity Widens
chapters.description: Equanimity is presented as a way of widening attention, softening tunnel vision, and making room for what is actually present.

chapters.title: Acceptance in Practice
chapters.description: He outlines recognition, allowing, non-judging, and non-attachment as a practical way to stay with sadness and grasping without acting them out.

chapters.title: Relationship and Dependence
chapters.description: Retreat, regression, and the wish for safety reveal how dependence brings control impulses, fear, and the need to revise old stories.

chapters.title: Compassion Without Control
chapters.description: A story of former monastics and a final distinction between compassion and control lead into a quiet close about caring, helplessness, and stillness.

title: Guided Meditation: Surrendering the Territory of Me
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with simple sensory attention and gradually widens into a softer relationship with body, sound, and awareness. Matthew Brensilver repeatedly returns to the felt sense of self as a territory we patrol, then invites a gentler letting go of that guarding and a more intimate experience of spaciousness. The closing instructions emphasize non-preference: meeting contraction and ease alike without making them a problem.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing the defended sense of self and resting in non-preferential awareness.

chapters.title: Settling on sound
chapters.description: Attention opens with the faintest sound and the edge between hearing and silence.

chapters.title: Soft gaze inward
chapters.description: The practice shifts to resting visual attention in the darkness or brightness behind closed eyes.

chapters.title: Weight and ease
chapters.description: Instructions return again and again to grounded bodily sensation, heaviness, and simple restful alertness.

chapters.title: The guarded territory
chapters.description: Matthew names the sense of self as a protected boundary and invites recognition of the effort to patrol it.

chapters.title: Lay down your arms
chapters.description: The defense of self and world is softened, and the practice turns toward relaxing resistance.

chapters.title: Contracting and opening
chapters.description: He points to self as a contractive force and then to the expansive side where self and not-self are made of the same elements.

chapters.title: Life happening
chapters.description: The meditation broadens into allowing life to be experienced as happening, without centering everything on 'me.'

chapters.title: No preference
chapters.description: The talk turns toward loving preferences and control without taking them seriously, and toward peace that does not depend on phenomena.

chapters.title: Closing reflection
chapters.description: Matthew briefly cites Shinzen Young on non-preference, then ends by inviting an intuitive sense of self as a defended boundary.

title: Dharma Supports Relationships, Relationships Help Us Get Freer
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores how Dharma practice can support relationships without turning them into projects, and how relationships can, in turn, reveal where we are still not free. He moves between personal stories, contemporary psychology, and Buddhist teaching to argue for love, ethical care, and clarity without promises that life will cooperate. The talk stays grounded in the tension between intimacy and freedom, ending with a quiet reminder that we need both a lighter touch and a wider view.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on how practice can deepen love and loosen clinging, without making relationships into a spiritual transaction.

chapters.title: Opening cautions
chapters.description: He begins by naming the sensitivity of the topic and asking listeners to hear the talk in light of their own history and circumstances.

chapters.title: Practice without bargaining
chapters.description: Matthew contrasts sincere practice with transactional striving, using Joseph Goldstein’s warning against practicing for prizes and the idea of preparing the heart to love well.

chapters.title: Conditions, not guarantees
chapters.description: He says Dharma does not promise that things will work out, but that it helps create conditions where honesty, care, and beautiful accidents are more likely.

chapters.title: Truth and relationship
chapters.description: A story about a friend’s vow leads into the question of whether relationship is mainly for mutual support or also a place to wake up.

chapters.title: Freedom and attachment
chapters.description: He weighs the monastic ideal of freedom independent of conditions against lay life, and describes the willingness to love what cannot be controlled.

chapters.title: Belonging and isolation
chapters.description: Drawing on psychology, Tony Kushner, Ajahn Sucitto, and recent social trends, he argues that human beings are deeply relational and increasingly isolated.

chapters.title: Sangha and non-harming
chapters.description: Matthew turns to the Buddha, harmony, and the precepts, describing Dharma as a way of becoming a safer, less defensive presence for others.

chapters.title: Love, clarity, and fog
chapters.description: A quoted graduation address on love leads into the need for moral strength, clarity, and the capacity to stay present when interpersonal pain clouds the mind.

chapters.title: One foot out
chapters.description: He closes with Nagarjuna and the emptiness of views, suggesting that practice helps us loosen possessiveness so we can love more lightly and meet others with care.

title: Landing in the Breath
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (Big Bear Retreat Center) All phenomena tend to be experienced as possibilities. Mindfulness is about putting all our hope in the present moment.
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet meditation on how the mind habitually turns present experience into a search for future safety, meaning, and self-confirmation. He begins with a body scan and then moves into a wider teaching on possibility, uncertainty, and the relief of returning to the actual breath. The latter part of the talk turns practical, with guidance for sitting and walking practice, including how to work with boredom, attention, and shifting anchors without forcing the mind.
short_summary: A grounded retreat talk on settling into bodily experience instead of reading every moment as a message about the future.

chapters.title: Arriving in the body
chapters.description: The talk opens with a gradual softening of the face, shoulders, belly, and the urge to perform or prove anything.

chapters.title: Possibility in experience
chapters.description: Matthew describes how sensations, thoughts, and emotions are quickly read as signs about where life is going.

chapters.title: Landing in the breath
chapters.description: He redirects attention from interpretation to the actuality of breathing and the simplicity of intimate contact.

chapters.title: Loose ends and uncertainty
chapters.description: The practice broadens to notice points within the breath cycle and to meet worry, loose ends, and uncertainty with equanimity.

chapters.title: Hope in this moment
chapters.description: A central refrain develops: caring for the future can mean placing all hope in the present moment rather than endlessly projecting forward.

chapters.title: Not overreading retreat
chapters.description: He speaks about first-day retreat intensity, resisting grand conclusions, and not turning each sit into a chapter of autobiography.

chapters.title: Walking with ease
chapters.description: The teaching shifts into walking practice, emphasizing a sustainable, relaxed awareness that can continue through movement and ordinary transitions.

chapters.title: Boredom and fresh anchors
chapters.description: Matthew brings in Stephen Levine on boredom, then shares a flexible sequence of attention anchors, including a suggestion attributed to Jesse Vega Fry, to keep practice alive and adaptable.

title: Guided Meditation: The Pull of Time
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a quiet guided meditation that moves from relaxing tension in the body into a broader reflection on care, attention, and the limits of control. The practice repeatedly returns to kindness toward pain and tightness, then widens into the ways wanting and fear shape attention and keep us oriented toward a future that never fully arrives. In the closing arc, he points to letting go, receiving the present moment, and recognizing that insight into the Four Noble Truths can be available right now.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing tension, softening grasping, and meeting the present moment without control.

chapters.title: Settling and Relaxing
chapters.description: The meditation begins by relaxing whatever can be relaxed and blessing what remains tense to stay as it is.

chapters.title: Tending the Body
chapters.description: Attention turns to bodily tightness or pain, with a suggestion to gently massage discomfort while staying in the flow of practice.

chapters.title: Care and Lineage
chapters.description: He frames self-care as an expression of loving-kindness and places the listener within a long lineage of love and care.

chapters.title: Practice and Human Longing
chapters.description: The talk widens to the wish to be free of suffering, the work of learning on the cushion, and the distinction between what is worthy of protection and what can be released.

chapters.title: Wanting and Fear
chapters.description: Brensilver reflects on how hope gets projected into the future and how renunciation loosens ownership, control, and our relationship to helplessness.

chapters.title: Letting Go to Receive
chapters.description: Using the phrase about not fooling Mother Nature, he emphasizes that we cannot control experience and must let go to receive this moment.

chapters.title: Insight on Offer
chapters.description: The meditation opens into a quieter teaching on recognizing what is happening, with the suggestion that insight can be available in any moment.

chapters.title: Not Missing It
chapters.description: The closing line returns to the simple task of doing our best not to miss the present opportunity.

title: Dharmette: Power and Safety
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on the strange mix of power, responsibility, and humility that comes with Dharma life. Drawing on a story about Ajahn Chah and the Thai forest tradition, he warns against ego inflation in teaching, then turns to sila, feedback, and communal safety as practical protections. The talk moves toward a quiet but forceful conclusion: practice can deepen our power, but it should also make us safer, more transparent, and more able to serve as a refuge for others.
short_summary: A reflective talk on how Dharma practice increases power while making humility, ethical conduct, and communal safety more important.

chapters.title: Why Power Matters
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the talk by naming a context of harm and unethical leadership, then frames the question of ethical conduct.

chapters.title: Ajahn Chah's Warning
chapters.description: He relays Kittisaro's account of Ajahn Chah advising monks not to identify as teachers, but to remain practitioners first.

chapters.title: Ripples of Goodness
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on how practice and teaching spread across time, and how one moment of goodness can reverberate for decades.

chapters.title: Dharma as Response
chapters.description: He turns to the Thai forest custom of requesting a Dhamma talk, using it to show that teaching should answer a real need rather than become performance.

chapters.title: Unpossessable Dharma
chapters.description: The talk develops the theme that Dharma, goodness, and even our roles cannot be owned, and that surrender and softness are necessary.

chapters.title: Feedback and Safety
chapters.description: Matthew emphasizes that we cannot fully see ourselves, so honest feedback and shared calibration are essential for communal safety.

chapters.title: Power and Prestige
chapters.description: He returns to the language of power and prestige, arguing that one cannot perform Dharma but must become it in a way that remains safe and ethical.

chapters.title: A Quiet Trace
chapters.description: The talk closes by linking insight into emptiness with safety, then asking listeners how they will use their power and make their hearts a refuge.

title: Guided Meditation: Relax and Let go
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation settles first into posture and deeper breathing, then moves toward a quieter willingness to stop trying to solve life. Matthew Brensilver emphasizes surrender, rest, and receiving the breath more fully, with a brief image from Thich Nhat Hanh and a nod to the Three Refuges as sources of soothing. The latter part becomes a long counting practice, steadying attention through repetition and simplicity.
short_summary: A quiet guided meditation on relaxing, surrendering control, and resting in the breath, ending with extended counting practice.

chapters.title: Arriving to meditate
chapters.description: The meditation opens with a brief welcome and a turn into practice.

chapters.title: Posture and breath
chapters.description: Attention settles into the body, posture, and a few deeper breaths.

chapters.title: Letting settle
chapters.description: A Thich Nhat Hanh image supports the sense of the body sinking and settling.

chapters.title: Releasing the urge
chapters.description: Matthew turns toward renouncing the impulse to solve life and the freedom of surrender.

chapters.title: What feels restful
chapters.description: The instruction narrows to whatever is already restful, including body, silence, and refuge.

chapters.title: Resting with practice
chapters.description: The repeated invitation to rest continues, allowing the mood of ease to deepen.

chapters.title: Receiving the breath
chapters.description: The practice shifts back to the breath, including its beginnings and endings and the suggestion to place attention elsewhere if helpful.

chapters.title: Counting practice
chapters.description: The talk moves into a long sustained counting sequence, using repetition to steady awareness and reduce effort.

title: Dharmette: Courage and Fear
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on courage as something distinct from general effort, and as a practice that is often shadowed by fear. He traces how fear narrows attention, distorts cognition, and makes letting go feel unsafe, then turns toward the way spiritual practice asks for honesty, surrender, and the willingness to meet uncertainty. The talk closes by connecting courage to virtue, the Buddhist perfections, and a quiet question about what love looks like when it is less afraid.
short_summary: A grounded reflection on how fear shapes the mind, and why courage is needed for letting go, self-honesty, and moral bravery.

chapters.title: Opening to courage
chapters.description: Matthew introduces courage as the day’s theme and speaks candidly about his own temperament and limited confidence teaching on it.

chapters.title: Fear at the threshold
chapters.description: He describes courage as disorienting because it asks us to step toward what we cannot fully predict and to accept possible pain or change.

chapters.title: Faith and the path
chapters.description: The talk turns to the spiritual path as a series of small acts of courage, where aspiration requires leaving old habits without knowing exactly what will replace them.

chapters.title: How fear works
chapters.description: Matthew explores fear as a deeply wired force that narrows cognition, makes clinging feel protective, and disrupts attention and samadhi.

chapters.title: Groundless moments
chapters.description: He names the courage needed to face suffering, change, and the moments when the ground feels unstable or entirely groundless.

chapters.title: Terror and waiting
chapters.description: Using the example of being lost in the woods, he suggests that sometimes the wisest response is not to force a solution but to stay still and let help arrive.

chapters.title: Fear of suffering
chapters.description: Matthew distinguishes sensitivity to suffering from fear of suffering, and notices how the wish to control pain can quietly shape practice.

chapters.title: Shared courage
chapters.description: He reflects on the courage he sees in others, invokes Aquinas on courage as foundational to virtue, and gathers the talk toward moral bravery.

chapters.title: Love less afraid
chapters.description: The closing question asks what love looks like when fear loosens, ending with a brief exhortation to keep going.

title: Guided Meditation: Relaxing the tension called 'me'
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation invites a softening around the felt tension of selfing, moving from settling the body into a careful inquiry into how control is maintained through feeling, thought, and image. Matthew Brensilver repeatedly returns to surrender as a simple laying down of strategy, and toward the end he emphasizes relaxing the contraction that accompanies uncertainty. The practice stays quiet and direct, with the instruction to notice what can relax and to allow the rest to remain.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing the contraction of self and releasing the impulse to control experience.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: After a brief welcome, the practice begins by finding posture and arriving in stillness.

chapters.title: Touching Tranquility
chapters.description: He points to the muscle memory of Dharma practice and suggests recollecting tranquility and openheartedness.

chapters.title: Relax What Can
chapters.description: The instruction narrows to relaxing whatever can be relaxed while allowing remaining tension to stay.

chapters.title: Self as Headquarters
chapters.description: The talk turns to the sense of self as a headquarters for control, and surrender as the self laying down.

chapters.title: Seeing the Mechanics
chapters.description: He names the body, mental images, words, and auditory thought as the tools that organize clinging and control.

chapters.title: From Control to Wakefulness
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts from acquiring and strategizing to simply being awake, without a hidden agenda.

chapters.title: Contraction and Uncertainty
chapters.description: Near the end, he asks listeners to notice self-contraction as weight and to relax the uncertainty that fuels control.

chapters.title: Habitualizing Impermanence
chapters.description: The closing line gestures toward habituating to anicca as the practice continues.

title: Dharmette: Surrender
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on surrender as a quiet but central part of practice: not resignation, but a willing release of control, habit, and the need to know. He moves from Leonard Cohen and St. Augustine into a Buddhist account of mindfulness, samadhi, and insight as forms of letting the self soften. The talk stays grounded in ordinary practice, where surrender becomes a way to meet experience honestly and find a different kind of power.
short_summary: A quiet talk on surrender as the release of control that allows mindfulness, insight, and deeper trust to mature.

chapters.title: Cohen and the search
chapters.description: Matthew opens with Leonard Cohen as an image of a life where striving gives way and even the search dissolves.

chapters.title: Restless heart
chapters.description: He turns to St. Augustine to evoke longing, humility, and the wish to be enlarged and remade by what is greater than the self.

chapters.title: Surrender in Dharma
chapters.description: Matthew reframes surrender in Buddhist terms as yielding to the moment, love, not-self, and the path rather than to a deity.

chapters.title: Discernment and trust
chapters.description: He balances surrender with the Kalama Sutta’s emphasis on verification, while warning that surrender can be distorted by bad ideas, habits, or people.

chapters.title: The will to control
chapters.description: Using Carl Rogers and his own language, he describes how practice often serves the ego until a phase shift begins and control starts to loosen.

chapters.title: Changed by experience
chapters.description: Matthew explores the point when the ego runs out of moves and practice becomes an openness to being changed by the moment and the breath.

chapters.title: Not knowing and refuge
chapters.description: He links surrender with letting go of ownership, relinquishing stories that no longer serve, and resting in groundlessness without losing dignity.

chapters.title: Gateway to absorption
chapters.description: Near the end, he describes how effort can become agitation at the threshold of samadhi and jhana, where trust in silence and the present becomes essential.

chapters.title: True power
chapters.description: Matthew closes by saying that the path still cultivates power, but the deepest power appears through surrender rather than control.

title: Guided Meditation: Stillness and the Dissolution of the Predictive Mind
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that begins with settling the body and gradually shifts toward the breath as a simple object of awareness. He links relaxation to a softer relationship with dukkha, then moves into a teaching on how meditation loosens the mind’s habit of predicting the next moment and imagining continuity. The talk closes by pointing toward samadhi as a quiet dissolution of that predictive architecture and a more direct falling into the present.
short_summary: A guided meditation on bodily settling, the breath, and how stillness can dissolve the mind’s habit of predicting the next moment.

chapters.title: Opening and settling in
chapters.description: Matthew greets the group and invites everyone into practice with a quiet, communal tone.

chapters.title: Doing much less
chapters.description: He frames meditation as a response to life feeling overwhelming, a way of doing less and becoming open to experience.

chapters.title: Softening the body
chapters.description: The practice turns to releasing tension in the face, jaw, shoulders, and belly, with repeated reminders to soften and let go.

chapters.title: Grounding and openness
chapters.description: He includes the hips, pelvis, feet, and hands, emphasizing a sense of energy moving through the body without obstruction.

chapters.title: Breath and aliveness
chapters.description: The breath becomes the main object of awareness, and the talk settles into enjoying simplicity and the pleasure of breathing.

chapters.title: Predicting the future
chapters.description: The teaching shifts from bodily calm to the mind’s subtle habit of leaning forward with expectations and projections.

chapters.title: Samadhi and craving
chapters.description: Matthew describes samadhi as a dissolution of predictive architecture and connects craving with the word 'next.'

chapters.title: Falling into presence
chapters.description: He softens the assumption that 'I am next,' encouraging a direct return to the present moment and a release of self-making.

title: Guided Meditation: Much Becomes Incidental
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet guided meditation centered on listening to silence and letting the usual mental inventory become incidental. The practice repeatedly returns to receiving the moment without project-making, self-management, or the need to solve experience. As the talk unfolds, surrender is framed not as passivity but as a temporary release of control in which even difficulty can be held more lightly.
short_summary: A guided meditation on listening to silence, releasing control, and letting much of ordinary mental life become incidental.

chapters.title: Listening to silence
chapters.description: The meditation begins by inviting attention to silence itself, even when there is noise around it.

chapters.title: What becomes incidental
chapters.description: Pleasant and unpleasant feeling, autobiography, and fixed ideas are allowed to recede for a little while.

chapters.title: Receiving the moment
chapters.description: The teaching settles into the simple stance that receiving the present moment is enough.

chapters.title: No projects, no building
chapters.description: Brensilver emphasizes dropping the impulse to build, manage, or improve experience.

chapters.title: Surrender and love
chapters.description: Surrender is described as the dissolution of volitional energy, with love named as the one thing worth building.

chapters.title: Receiving space
chapters.description: The practice widens into a spacious resting in which space seems to receive space.

chapters.title: A new chance to let go
chapters.description: The talk returns to the ordinary urge to medicate feelings and thought, and frames the session as another opportunity to release control.

chapters.title: Emerging unchanged
chapters.description: The closing notes suggest that life will still be there afterward, the same and yet slightly different after practice.

title: Dharmette: Self-Esteem Preoccupation as Symptom
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on self-esteem as a symptom rather than a solution, drawing on social psychology and Dharma to question the habit of measuring worth. He moves from scientific conversation and familiar retreat anecdotes into a quieter argument that suffering, fear, and need deprivation generate self-preoccupation, and that practice can soften that grip. The talk closes by pointing toward ordinariness, ethical living, refuge, and the possibility that it is enough simply to be a person.
short_summary: A short Dharma reflection on self-esteem, selfing, and the relief of becoming ordinary.

chapters.title: Opening the conversation
chapters.description: Matthew begins with a few light remarks about the Dharma path and its conversation with science.

chapters.title: Science and Dharma
chapters.description: He recalls a Mind and Life conference with Richard Davidson to show how scientific and contemplative approaches are learning from each other.

chapters.title: Self-esteem as symptom
chapters.description: Using Brown and Ryan, he frames self-esteem preoccupation as a sign of unmet needs rather than a healthy foundation.

chapters.title: Not-self without idealism
chapters.description: He turns to not-self as a way of seeing things as they are, not as a moral project or spiritual achievement.

chapters.title: Ordinary worthiness
chapters.description: A memory of Jack Kornfield leads into a gentler understanding of dignity rooted in ordinariness rather than specialness.

chapters.title: Selfing and pain
chapters.description: Matthew argues that selfing is an effect of pain, fear, and grasping, not a fixed cause standing apart from them.

chapters.title: Thawing the wound
chapters.description: He describes a gradual thaw in which heaviness, shame, and unmet need are met more tenderly.

chapters.title: Ethics and refuge
chapters.description: Ethical conduct and taking refuge are presented as ways life becomes less self-centered and more incidental.

chapters.title: Just being a person
chapters.description: He closes with Garfield's distinction between self and person, suggesting it is enough to play our roles without proving who we are.

title: Dharmette: Expansiveness of Awe
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on awe as a kind of pleasant disorientation that widens experience rather than narrowing it. He moves from nature and virtuosity to moral beauty, the history of the Dharma, and the spaciousness that can appear when suffering and convention are seen more lightly. The talk closes by linking awe with freedom, love, and a less claustrophobic way of living.
short_summary: A quiet reflection on awe as a widening of mind, heart, and possible ways of living.

chapters.title: Disorientation and Awe
chapters.description: He begins by reframing disorientation as something Dharma practice can acclimatize us to, then introduces awe as a pleasant but slightly unsettling widening of mind.

chapters.title: What Awe Does
chapters.description: Using Dacher Keltner’s definition, he describes awe as encounter with something vast and notes its links to generosity, nature, art, and moral elevation.

chapters.title: Nature, Skill, Goodness
chapters.description: He gives examples from mountains, Simone Biles, and ordinary goodness to show how awe can come through landscape, virtuosity, and moral beauty.

chapters.title: Beyond Claustrophobia
chapters.description: Awe is presented as a way out of the claustrophobic self, opening a broader sense of life, practice, and lineage.

chapters.title: Dharma Across Time
chapters.description: He turns to gratitude for the Dharma and recalls an early retreat experience that made the vastness of practice feel emotionally overwhelming in a good way.

chapters.title: Closeness of Peace
chapters.description: Awe becomes tied to the proximity of peace, the preciousness of human life, and the strangeness of simply being here at all.

chapters.title: Freedom and Convention
chapters.description: Drawing on existential psychotherapy and the fetter of attachment to convention, he asks how to live when so much of what feels solid is self-made.

chapters.title: Stars and Smallness
chapters.description: A retreat memory under the stars becomes a meditation on vast space and time, making personal worries feel small and life feel more open.

chapters.title: Living in Awe
chapters.description: He closes by suggesting that we do not fully acclimate to awe and that its openness can remain a living question rather than a settled state.

title: Guided Meditation: Reverence & Renunciation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that frames practice as both reverence and renunciation. He invites a posture of sanctity without strain, then guides attention to the breath as a way of putting down multitasking, worry, story-making, and clinging. The talk widens into a quiet reflection on lineage, sincerity, and the way letting go can simplify experience and make breathing more intimate.
short_summary: A guided meditation on reverence, renunciation, and meeting this moment with less clinging and more intimacy.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: A brief welcome and opening transition into shared practice.

chapters.title: Posture as Reverence
chapters.description: He frames posture as an open heart toward the world, with sanctity and relaxation held together.

chapters.title: Breath and Softening
chapters.description: The practice moves into the belly breath, encouraging a fuller and slower rhythm.

chapters.title: Sacred Lineage
chapters.description: He reminds listeners that even familiar meditation is part of a larger lineage of wisdom and compassion.

chapters.title: What to Put Down
chapters.description: Renunciation is defined as a simple, non-moralistic recognition of what can be set aside for now.

chapters.title: Story and Hope
chapters.description: He points to the tendency to proliferate around sense contact and to postpone life into the future.

chapters.title: Clinging Simplifies
chapters.description: The talk turns to the false promises of clinging and the relief that comes when grasping is released.

chapters.title: Resolution and Bells
chapters.description: He names samadhi as a momentary resolution of ambivalence, then the meditation closes in silence with bells.

title: Dharmette: Grief as a Facet of Dharma
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers grief as something deeper than a passing emotion: a companion to wakefulness, love, and ethical growth. He moves through the Buddha’s life, the First Noble Truth, and contemporary psychology to suggest that Dharma practice does not erase grief so much as transmute it. The talk stays close to the lived experience of change, loss, and the widening of the heart.
short_summary: A quiet Dharma reflection on grief as an inseparable part of awakening, love, and ethical maturation.

chapters.title: Dharma and tension
chapters.description: He begins by framing Dharma as a set of teachings held in dynamic tension, introducing grief as one indispensable side of practice.

chapters.title: Grief as irreducible
chapters.description: He names grief as the space between freedom and suffering, and argues that even deep practice does not eliminate it.

chapters.title: The Buddha and loss
chapters.description: Using Leonard Cohen and the Buddha’s own life story, he shows how wakefulness and loss belong together rather than cancel each other out.

chapters.title: Not a pathology
chapters.description: He turns to anthropodenial and explains that grief is not simply an afflictive emotion to be removed, though it can become stuck or misleading.

chapters.title: Practice and growing pains
chapters.description: He links grief to grief-informed ethics, the pain of letting go, and the growing pains that come with deeper compassion and change.

chapters.title: Facing dukkha
chapters.description: Drawing on the First Noble Truth and Shelley Taylor’s work on positive illusions, he contrasts truthfulness with self-deception and the agitation it creates.

chapters.title: Grieving to change
chapters.description: James Baldwin helps him distinguish real transformation from merely expressive sorrow, emphasizing that grief can accompany genuine change.

chapters.title: Grief into love
chapters.description: He closes by describing grief as pregnant with bodhicitta, a process of purification in which pain is not far from love.

title: Guided Meditation: All Phenomena are Friendly
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a guided meditation that steadily loosens the pressure of self-judgment and future-minded striving. He invites listeners to set down the habit of making the self into an object, rest in the breath, and become less intimidated by uncertainty. The practice gradually opens into a quieter Dharma perspective: experience can be met more softly, even when it hurts.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing self-pressure, resting in breath, and meeting experience with the view that all phenomena are friendly.

chapters.title: Arrival and settling
chapters.description: A brief welcome and orientation into practice, with an invitation to find a posture that balances alertness and relaxation.

chapters.title: Letting go of selfing
chapters.description: The meditation turns toward dropping the habit of assessing oneself and forgetting familiar stories about who we are.

chapters.title: Breath and fresh space
chapters.description: Guidance opens into breathing and bodily ease, encouraging a simpler relationship to the present moment.

chapters.title: Pressure to perform
chapters.description: Matthew explores how self-concern accumulates and how relaxing can soften the drive to prove, perform, or become.

chapters.title: Uncertainty and release
chapters.description: The talk names anicca and the burden of trying to manage every future self, then encourages resting in the open space of this breath.

chapters.title: Precision without tension
chapters.description: The meditation becomes more specific about the breath, with attention to beginnings, endings, and the pause of release.

chapters.title: All phenomena friendly
chapters.description: The central phrase is repeated and then clarified as a Dharma view: everything can be met as friendly, even when some experiences hurt.

title: Dharmette: Humor in the Dharma
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a short Dharma talk on humor and playfulness as part of practice, arguing that seriousness is not the same as sincerity and that insight can make room for laughter. He moves through personal stories, references to Thai Forest teachers, and quotes from George Saunders, Freud, Nietzsche, and Winnicott to show how humor can reveal truth, loosen self-consciousness, and deepen friendship. The talk closes with an account of crying and laughing at once, suggesting that grief, joy, and peace can co-occur without canceling each other out.
short_summary: A brief Dharma talk on how humor, play, and lightness can coexist with depth, grief, and insight.

chapters.title: A playful fake list
chapters.description: He introduces the idea of a personal, unofficial list of Dharma factors, setting up humor and playfulness as the first theme.

chapters.title: Captain Buzzkill
chapters.description: A story about being called out for dampening excitement leads into his claim that Dharma can become overly serious when ego takes over.

chapters.title: Seriousness and lightness
chapters.description: He argues that practice can make experience more spacious and less self-bound, which opens the door to humor.

chapters.title: Truth told quickly
chapters.description: George Saunders and Freud are used to suggest that humor and shared laughter can reveal truth and deeper common ground.

chapters.title: Learning to play
chapters.description: Nietzsche’s idea of mature play frames Dharma as a way of recovering ease, non-vigilance, and less self-consciousness.

chapters.title: Therapy and play
chapters.description: Winnicott’s account of therapy as shared play is used to widen the point: healing often means moving from rigidity into playfulness.

chapters.title: Playing with self
chapters.description: He explores how practice can loosen the conventions of self, letting humor meet shame, pain, and defensiveness without denial.

chapters.title: Crying and laughing
chapters.description: A breathing-workshop story brings the talk to a close with crying, laughter, and peace coexisting in one porous experience.

title: Guided Meditation: Arriving and Opening
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a gentle guided meditation centered on arriving, softening self-conscious effort, and letting the body be as it is. The practice moves from settling posture and attention, to checking for places that need patience or care, to resting with the breath and the felt sense of the body. He closes by framing the practice in terms of relinquishment, true nourishment, and a quiet metta-like response to agitation.
short_summary: A quiet guided meditation on arriving, softening, and finding ease through relinquishment.

chapters.title: Arriving Gently
chapters.description: He begins by encouraging an unhurried arrival, without forcing posture or shaping attention.

chapters.title: Sensing What Needs Care
chapters.description: The practice widens to notice anything in the body or mind that may need awareness, patience, or softening.

chapters.title: Relaxing Self-Image
chapters.description: He points toward body sensations that hurt, and invites a release of self-conscious tension and fixed ideas about the body.

chapters.title: Breathing With Safety
chapters.description: The guidance settles on breathing with whatever alarm or resistance arises, staying close to the beginning and ending of each breath.

chapters.title: Relinquishment and Nourishment
chapters.description: He turns to the Buddha's teaching that peace comes through relinquishment and Ajahn Chah's image of 'food for the heart.'

chapters.title: Extended Silent Practice
chapters.description: A long stretch of quiet practice follows, with the meditation continuing in silence.

chapters.title: Meeting Agitation
chapters.description: He returns to naming restlessness, finding something stable, and responding to ill-will with a brief, direct metta.

title: Dharmette: Question about Etiquette Approaching Teachers
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver responds to a listener’s question about etiquette in approaching meditation teachers by shifting the focus from rules to relationship. He frames teaching as service and devotion, then explores projection, transference, and the ways teachers and students can use contact to see the mind more clearly. The talk closes with a careful distinction between wanting to be loved and wanting to be understood, and with sincerity as the heart of genuine Dharma exchange.
short_summary: A clear, reflective talk about how students and teachers meet in sincerity rather than etiquette or ego.

chapters.title: Question and framing
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the question about approaching teachers and uses a comic reference to open the issue of approval and need.

chapters.title: Teaching as service
chapters.description: He suggests teachers want very little from students, and describes teaching as a practice of compassion, devotion, and care for the Dharma.

chapters.title: No fixed etiquette
chapters.description: Matthew argues that teachers should meet students flexibly and create unpressured space rather than impose a code of conduct.

chapters.title: Projection and transference
chapters.description: He turns to how teacher relationships activate old patterns, including psychoanalytic ideas of projection and transference.

chapters.title: Relationship as healing
chapters.description: Matthew says these dynamics can help reveal need and longing, and that spiritual maturity includes tolerating ambivalence.

chapters.title: What teachers want
chapters.description: He identifies sincerity as the main quality teachers look for, describing it as willingness to examine the questions that support a life.

chapters.title: Loved or understood
chapters.description: Matthew distinguishes the need to be loved from the wish to be understood, and says he is most moved when student and teacher meet in open Dharma rather than role.

title: Talk: Spiritual Friendship and Sila as Our Practice
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on how practice is shaped not just by private effort, but by spiritual friendship, Sangha, and the ways other people enter the mind. He moves from the Buddha’s teaching that spiritual friendship is the whole of the holy life into a close exploration of sila: ethical conduct as embodied care, self-respect, and a life with less regret. The talk also turns toward retreat, moral sanity in a difficult world, and the quiet ways relationship helps us see ourselves more clearly.
short_summary: A talk on spiritual friendship, Sangha, and sila as the relational ground of practice and moral clarity.

chapters.title: Spiritual friendship
chapters.description: He opens with the Buddha and Ananda exchange to argue that awakening is fundamentally relational, not a solo project.

chapters.title: Mind is permeable
chapters.description: Using the Dhammapada and everyday examples, he describes the mind as conditioned, open, and shaped by what we let in.

chapters.title: Sangha as support
chapters.description: He tells a teaching story about learning to appreciate Sangha as the care that holds practice together.

chapters.title: Warm hand to warm hand
chapters.description: Drawing on Ajahn Sucitto, he explores transmission, heart resonance, and how practice is learned through living contact.

chapters.title: Not escape from life
chapters.description: He returns to retreat and the pressures of the wider world, stressing that practice is not withdrawal but a way of entering life more fully.

chapters.title: Training through habits
chapters.description: With William James and a mentor’s correction about kindness versus niceness, he shows how ethical life is built through repeated habits and honest care.

chapters.title: Seeing ourselves clearly
chapters.description: He develops the theme that relationship reveals blind spots, helping us understand our impact, our suffering, and the roots of empathy.

chapters.title: Sila and non-regret
chapters.description: He closes by linking moral expansion to sila as a life of reverence, carefulness, and non-regret that leaves less trace behind.

title: Evening Reflections
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: In this short evening reflection, Matthew Brensilver invites a simple shift of orientation: to appreciate one’s own goodness without turning that into self-indulgence. He explores the tension around self-love, deserving, shame, and pride, and then points toward a more basic sense of worthiness that comes before those judgments. The talk closes by encouraging that felt sense of goodness to accompany the listener into the night, sleep, and dreams.
short_summary: A brief reflection on self-love, worthiness, and letting a sense of goodness carry into the night.

chapters.title: Appreciating Goodness
chapters.description: The talk opens by saying it is not self-indulgent to appreciate your own goodness, even briefly.

chapters.title: Loving Yourself
chapters.description: The reflection extends the same invitation into self-love and notes that this can feel unfamiliar or charged.

chapters.title: Beyond Debates
chapters.description: The speaker names the inner arguments about deserving and undeserving, along with the pull of pride and shame.

chapters.title: A Deeper Worthiness
chapters.description: Rather than staying with moral judgments, the talk points to a kind of worthiness that comes before deserving and undeserving.

chapters.title: Carry It Into Sleep
chapters.description: The practice becomes an evening blessing: let that goodness follow you into bed, into the mind, and into dreams.

chapters.title: Closing Thanks
chapters.description: The talk ends quietly with thanks.

title: Guided Meditation: Mostly Quiet Sit
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This mostly quiet guided sit begins with a simple invitation to stop fighting awareness and rest with what cannot be stopped. It then widens into several possible anchors—breath, body, soundscape, feeling tone, and even auditory thinking—while repeatedly softening the sense of effort and self-management. In the last portion, Matthew turns toward a poetic line about a burden that remains heavy even when understood as empty, framing practice as careful attention and love rather than dismissal.
short_summary: A quiet guided meditation on resting with awareness, softening effort, and including sounds, thoughts, and difficulty with care.

chapters.title: Rest with awareness
chapters.description: The meditation opens by asking listeners to stop fighting awareness and rest with what remains present.

chapters.title: Choose an anchor
chapters.description: He offers several possible anchors, including breath, body, soundscape, and hedonic tone.

chapters.title: Ease the selfing
chapters.description: The practice turns toward noticing how self-identified effort, pressure, and patrol can accumulate in the field of awareness.

chapters.title: Soften, widen, relax
chapters.description: A long, repeated period of instruction settles into simple cues to soften, widen, and relax without pushing experience.

chapters.title: Include inner sound
chapters.description: He suggests listening to auditory thinking as sound, without making it special or pushing it away.

chapters.title: Heavy becomes light
chapters.description: The closing teaching uses a poetic line about burden, emptiness, and love to frame difficult experience as something to be held carefully.

title: Talk: Knowing, Not-Knowing, Selfing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores Dharma as a disciplined movement between knowing and not knowing: learning enough to see suffering clearly, while loosening the need to control experience with certainty. He connects self-knowledge to dialogue, sangha, and the willingness to let familiar identity become less solid. The talk gradually shifts from ideas about practice into a close examination of how selfing is built from habit, feeling tone, and subtle assumptions.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on how self-knowledge depends on both clear seeing and a release of certainty.

chapters.title: Knowing and Not Knowing
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the talk as a dance between knowing and not knowing, and frames selfing as something shaped by grasping and ignorance.

chapters.title: Safety and Power
chapters.description: He develops knowing and not knowing as complementary qualities, showing how practice needs both precision and an openness that does not cover over mystery.

chapters.title: Dialogue and Inquiry
chapters.description: Using Agnes Callard, he argues that self-understanding requires other people, honest questioning, and the courage to unsettle fixed answers.

chapters.title: Narrative Fractures
chapters.description: He turns to the ways self-narratives break down, and how practice reveals that our summaries of ourselves are always incomplete.

chapters.title: Ignorance and Dukkha
chapters.description: Drawing on the Buddha's teachings, he links ignorance to unskillful qualities, the Four Noble Truths, and the need to understand conditioning in order to be free.

chapters.title: Learning What Matters
chapters.description: He contrasts ordinary learning with Dharma learning, quoting Yeats, Ajahn Chah, and the Dhammapada to show that practice means loosening the mind's grip on security and certainty.

chapters.title: Renunciation of Control
chapters.description: Brensilver describes how compulsive knowing is driven by fear, and how surrender is the dissolution of volitional energies rather than a strategy for getting somewhere.

chapters.title: The Two Selves
chapters.description: He distinguishes narrative and sensorial selfing, then shows how the self becomes more visible when the mind gets quiet and what was transparent begins to look opaque.

chapters.title: Listening Through Identity
chapters.description: With Nisargadatta and Ajahn Sucitto, he emphasizes deep listening to feeling tone, mood, and habit energies, ending with a gentler view of self and more compassion for its arising.

title: Evening Reflections - A Poem
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reads and reflects on a poem about impermanence, using a mountain retreat in New Mexico as the setting for a quiet confrontation with time, fragility, and change. The talk moves from the feel of austere landscape to a direct meditation on mortality, then toward a more spacious relationship to fear and grief. It ends with a simple emphasis on remembering, letting go, and allowing the warmth that can come with loss.
short_summary: A brief reflection on impermanence that moves from mountain landscape to mortality, fear, and the warmth of grief.

chapters.title: Mountain Austerity
chapters.description: He opens with the feel of a retreat landscape and the way beauty and severity sharpen attention.

chapters.title: A Poem Appears
chapters.description: Matthew names and introduces the poem that will shape the rest of the reflection.

chapters.title: Stability Crumbles
chapters.description: The poem's lines on instability and non-existence are read as a direct challenge to ordinary assumptions of permanence.

chapters.title: Fear Wakes Up
chapters.description: He turns to the bodily force of impermanence, linking it to a childhood memory of dusk and to ancestral fear.

chapters.title: Aliveness Opens
chapters.description: What first felt like fear becomes a larger sense of aliveness as change is seen more clearly.

chapters.title: Remembering and Letting Go
chapters.description: The talk closes with a simple invitation to remember impermanence, release clinging, and allow grief to be felt.

title: Guided Meditation: Life without Words
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a quiet guided meditation centered on the question of what life feels like without words. The practice moves from noticing the mind’s urge to narrativize experience, through working with urgency and craving, into a long stretch of silence and a gradual turn toward stillness. Near the end, he explicitly frames the meditation in terms of relinquishing ownership and self-making, invoking the Buddha’s instruction to give up what is not yours.
short_summary: A guided meditation that softens narration, meets urgency, and opens into stillness and not-self.

chapters.title: Opening question
chapters.description: The meditation begins by asking what life would feel like without words, setting the tone of inquiry and nonverbal attention.

chapters.title: Meaning and narration
chapters.description: He reflects on how words make meaning, and how practice can loosen the compulsion to turn each moment into a story.

chapters.title: Sense experience
chapters.description: Attention is guided toward raw sense experience and the habit of checking what is okay and not okay.

chapters.title: Fewer words
chapters.description: The instruction narrows toward breathing and repeated reminders to use fewer and fewer words.

chapters.title: Working with urgency
chapters.description: A pool of feeling and urgency is acknowledged, with an invitation to listen without being overtaken by it.

chapters.title: Silence pervades
chapters.description: A long silent stretch deepens the practice, including the reminder that nothing needs to be acquired, proved, or become anyone.

chapters.title: Give up ownership
chapters.description: The Buddha reference to giving up what is not yours opens into a repeated inquiry about ownership, answered with 'everything.'

chapters.title: Stillness as object
chapters.description: The practice turns to stillness directly, with repeated instructions to take stillness as the object and not go anywhere.

chapters.title: Closing and transition
chapters.description: The meditation ends by easing out of formal practice while staying with the body, the visual field, and a non-grasping attitude.

title: Talk: Whys and Hows of Effort - Energy & Bhava Tanha
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on effort as something steadied by trust, patience, and a clear sense of why we practice. He moves from the hidden way goodness accrues, to motivation rooted in benefit for others, to the tension between wholesome effort and craving for becoming. The talk closes by warning against spiritual striving and inviting a more receptive, willing relationship to practice.
short_summary: A grounded talk on effort, motivation, and how to practice without getting trapped in craving or self-construction.

chapters.title: Listening and trust
chapters.description: He opens by reflecting on how to listen to Dharma talks selectively, then turns to effort, patience, and trust as the mood that lets practice take root.

chapters.title: Practice that simply continues
chapters.description: Using Sharon Salzberg and Nietzsche, he describes practice as something we keep doing from faith in the path, even when results are not obvious.

chapters.title: Why make the effort
chapters.description: He asks what actually sustains effort, shifts from self-centered motivation to concern for others, and points to the way healing and love spill beyond the self.

chapters.title: Precious human life
chapters.description: He introduces the Tibetan contemplations, beginning with the preciousness and strangeness of being born at all as a source of reverence and motivation.

chapters.title: Against the stream
chapters.description: A long aside on cosmology gives way to the Buddhist point that conditioning pushes us toward habit, while practice asks for a critical stance and real energy to let go.

chapters.title: Open palm of release
chapters.description: He explores how effort becomes the energy to listen to our own wisdom, then turns toward receptivity, surrender, and willingness rather than willfulness.

chapters.title: Non-gaining practice
