chapters.title: Happiness Opens the View
chapters.description: Brensilver argues that a settled enough happiness often comes before insight, because well-being broadens attention and lets wisdom arise more easily.

chapters.title: Reinforcing Insight
chapters.description: He describes how insight becomes real through repeated reinforcement, the support of spiritual friends, and the reminder to keep practicing.

chapters.title: Practice It Again
chapters.description: The closing section encourages listeners to act from wisdom even when the feeling is not yet present, and to keep cultivating the conditions that let insight deepen.

title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties: Samadhi (4 of 5)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores samadhi as more than narrow concentration, describing it as a gathered, receptive, and internally unified mind. He moves through how samadhi relates to mindfulness, why it depends on letting go rather than force, and how it can support faith and deeper insight. The talk closes by suggesting that learning on a stable mind leaves a deeper mark and lasts longer in the body and practice.
short_summary: A quiet reflection on samadhi as receptive stability that supports faith, insight, and lasting learning.

chapters.title: What Samadhi Is
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces the five faculties and begins by asking how mindfulness and samadhi relate.

chapters.title: Not Separate Practices
chapters.description: He uses Thanissaro Bhikkhu and the Buddha’s teaching to argue that mindfulness and concentration are not opposed.

chapters.title: Attention and Receptivity
chapters.description: Shinzen Young’s three-part definition and images of spotlighting, aiming, and opening the hand frame samadhi as poised receptivity.

chapters.title: Aligned Inner Energies
chapters.description: He describes samadhi as a state where intentions align, impulses quiet, and the heart no longer needs to cope in the same way.

chapters.title: Faith Through Stillness
chapters.description: Samadhi is presented as a source of confidence in practice, showing that the path is real and not something only for others.

chapters.title: Letting Go, Not Forcing
chapters.description: He cautions against trying to cling into stillness and emphasizes that concentration is a pleasure born of letting go.

chapters.title: Ground for Insight
chapters.description: Brensilver turns to samadhi’s role in making the mind fertile for learning, so insight into impermanence and self can land more deeply.

chapters.title: Closing Encouragement
chapters.description: He offers the talk as something to take or leave, noting that people differ in how easily samadhi comes and ending with gentle encouragement to continue.

title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Mindfulness (3 of 5)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver uses this short dharmette to unpack what mindfulness is, and just as importantly, what it is not. After noting how the word can get overloaded or diluted, he turns to the Satipatthana Sutta and sketches mindfulness as the opposite of many common features of distress: rumination, fragmentation, avoidance, rigid self-stories, and moralism. The talk stays close to lived experience, ending with mindfulness as a more spacious, stable, less self-centered way of meeting what arises.
short_summary: A concise reflection on mindfulness as the opposite of distress, grounded in the Satipatthana Sutta and everyday experience.

chapters.title: Opening the topic
chapters.description: He begins by greeting the group and naming mindfulness as the final topic in a short sequence on the spiritual faculties.

chapters.title: A loaded word
chapters.description: Using Nietzsche’s image of words as pockets, he explains how mindfulness can gather many meanings and be stretched in unhelpful ways.

chapters.title: Satipatthana baseline
chapters.description: He turns to the classical definition of right mindfulness and frames it as something to understand through practice, not just definition.

chapters.title: How distress feels
chapters.description: He sketches shared features of distress: thoughts that feel absolute, rumination, narrowing of experience, and unstable attention.

chapters.title: What suffering does
chapters.description: The talk moves through more markers of distress, including arousal shifts, avoidance, low tolerance for pain, personalization, and rigid models of self and world.

chapters.title: Less fusion, more space
chapters.description: He describes mindfulness as reduced fusion with thought, de-reification, and a less intimidating inner world, linking this to equanimity.

chapters.title: Stability and tolerance
chapters.description: Mindfulness is presented as non-interference, attentional stability, and a growing capacity to stay present with charged experience.

chapters.title: Less self, more wakefulness
chapters.description: He closes with mindfulness as a relaxed, hypo-egoic openness in which the self is less central and awareness remains awake.

title: Guided Meditation: Beginning with Goodness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with the suggestion that practice can start from a felt sense of goodness already present in experience. Matthew Brensilver then repeatedly returns to the breath as a place of receiving, releasing, and settling into care without strain. The closing section brings the meditation into a larger view, suggesting that the sacred does not need to be manufactured and can be recognized in ordinary life.
short_summary: A guided meditation that roots practice in goodness, breath, and the quiet recognition that the sacred is already here.

chapters.title: Beginning with goodness
chapters.description: The meditation opens by framing Dharma as growing more sensitive to goodness in oneself, others, and the path.

chapters.title: Settling in the body
chapters.description: Listeners are invited to sense goodness running through the body and to use that as a support when the mind tightens.

chapters.title: Breath as nourishment
chapters.description: The practice turns toward the felt relief of breathing in and out, with attention to the body being nourished and released.

chapters.title: Relaxing vigilance
chapters.description: Matthew names the habit of hedging against the present and encourages a gentler trust in what is happening now.

chapters.title: Devoting ourselves here
chapters.description: The focus narrows to the breathing body, with an emphasis on clarity, precision, and presence without tension.

chapters.title: The sacred already present
chapters.description: The closing quotation reinforces the teaching that the sacred need not be manufactured, but can be recognized in ordinary experience.

title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Energy (2 of 5)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on the energy faculty as a middle way between forceful striving and passivity. He moves from a recent retreat theme of doing and non-doing into a careful look at how willpower can tangle with self-view, then offers a quieter definition of right effort as relaxed perseverance guided by wisdom. The talk closes by connecting habit, craving, and the energy needed to put down suffering’s causes rather than just endure their effects.
short_summary: A grounded reflection on energy in practice as effort guided by wisdom, not willfulness.

chapters.title: From Faith to Energy
chapters.description: He introduces the second faculty and situates it in the larger sequence of the five faculties.

chapters.title: Doing and Non-Doing
chapters.description: A recent retreat with Inez Friedman becomes a concrete frame for the balance between effortful practice and letting go.

chapters.title: The Buddha's Relentless Effort
chapters.description: He quotes the Buddha on sustained striving, then asks what kind of effort this actually points to.

chapters.title: When Effort Becomes Self
chapters.description: The talk turns toward the ways willfulness, conceit, and spiritual comparison can get woven into practice.

chapters.title: Witnessing the Limits
chapters.description: Matthew describes the pain that comes with self-harshness and the need to see where willpower fails.

chapters.title: Relaxed Perseverance
chapters.description: He offers underdoing, right effort with wisdom, and continuous awareness as a gentler form of energy.

chapters.title: Why Effort Matters
chapters.description: Effort is placed within the suttas and within ordinary life, where habits and neurosis often run on their own.

chapters.title: Habit and Craving
chapters.description: William James and Shantideva help explain how patterns repeat, why clinging feels reinforced, and why renunciation takes energy.

chapters.title: Loving the Causes Less
chapters.description: He closes by saying energy is often about loosening our attachment to the causes of suffering and sourcing effort from goodness.

title: Guided Meditation: Letting the Breath Come to You
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet guided meditation that begins by noticing what happens when we "sit together" and then shifts toward resting in what cannot be stopped. He gradually distinguishes the breath as a gentle object of attention while also emphasizing a broader openness in which awareness already receives everything. The practice returns again and again to the breath as something ordinary, nourishing, and not separate from the rest of life.
short_summary: A guided meditation on letting the breath be received naturally, while resting in open awareness and a wise, non-suspicious attitude toward experience.

chapters.title: Arriving Together
chapters.description: A brief welcome leads into the invitation to sit, settle, and notice what changes as practice begins.

chapters.title: What Meditation Does
chapters.description: He asks what tightens, what loosens, and what we try to do when we start meditating, then points to what cannot be stopped.

chapters.title: Resting In Openness
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward resting in what remains present already, rather than adding effort on top of it.

chapters.title: Open Awareness
chapters.description: He emphasizes receptivity and the difference between directing attention and forgetting the larger field of awareness.

chapters.title: Starting With Wise View
chapters.description: He names wise view as the place from which practice can launch and frames the session as another round of sitting together.

chapters.title: Breath As The Circle
chapters.description: The breath is introduced as a practical object of attention, like drawing a circle around sensations of breathing.

chapters.title: Receiving The Breath
chapters.description: The practice deepens into receiving the rhythm of breathing and recognizing that attending to the breath honors the rest of life.

chapters.title: Nothing Outside Is Suspicious
chapters.description: He widens the attitude around practice, saying that what lies outside the breath is not suspect and that all experience can be met innocently.

chapters.title: Gentle Inclining
chapters.description: The meditation closes by encouraging a soft, repeated inclining toward the breath and letting the mind take its cues from its cycles.

title: Dharmette: Five Spiritual Faculties - Faith (1 of 5)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver opens a five-part series on the spiritual faculties by focusing on faith, not as belief in fixed ideas, but as a confidence that grows out of suffering, experience, and practice. He moves from the doubt that first makes practice sincere, through scriptural and teaching references, to a steadier trust that feels like love, refuge, and a return to the heart's native ground. The talk closes with faith as a quiet ease that helps us breathe more easily in an unpredictable life.
short_summary: Matthew Brensilver explores faith as confidence born from suffering, experience, and a living relationship to practice.

chapters.title: Opening and Theme
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the series on the five spiritual faculties and names faith as the first topic.

chapters.title: Faith Begins in Doubt
chapters.description: He says practice often begins with the recognition that the old way of living will not work.

chapters.title: Suffering as Support
chapters.description: Matthew argues that suffering can become the condition that gives rise to faith and sincere practice.

chapters.title: Not Dogmatic Belief
chapters.description: Using the Buddha and Gil Fronsdal, he contrasts faith with fundamentalism and rigid views.

chapters.title: Trusting Experience
chapters.description: He cites Ajahn Amaro to describe faith as trusting the implications of experience rather than filling uncertainty with certainties.

chapters.title: Faith Like Falling In Love
chapters.description: Matthew compares commitment to the path with falling in love, something opaque, renewing, and not reducible to reasons.

chapters.title: Its Own Evidence
chapters.description: With Emerson and the language of refuge, he describes faith as self-authenticating and continually renewed.

chapters.title: Stable Yet Living
chapters.description: He reflects on how faith matures into a steadier threshold, bringing security, relaxation, and less self-absorption.

chapters.title: Ease and Consolation
chapters.description: The talk closes by describing faith as a quiet confidence that does not erase suffering, but offers solace and helps us keep going.

title: Non-Doing & Liberative Dependent Orignation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores non-doing as a real trajectory of Dharma practice, moving from effort and courage into a quieter logic of surrender. Using the Therigatha, Augustine, and several contemporary Dharma teachers, he traces how faith, joy, tranquility, concentration, disenchantment, and dispassion can unfold when willfulness relaxes. The talk gradually shifts from the felt work of practice to the possibility that liberation is less something manufactured than something allowed.
short_summary: A grounded talk on how practice can unfold through effort, then gradually through surrender, faith, and non-doing.

chapters.title: Opening with humility
chapters.description: Matthew begins with a playful anecdote and uses it to set a tone of honesty rather than performance.

chapters.title: Friendship and trust
chapters.description: He reads from the Therigatha on friendship, trust, and making the mind a companion on the path.

chapters.title: Effort and surrender
chapters.description: The talk turns to effort, courage, and the limits of trying to control practice, then opens into surrender.

chapters.title: Hands off the wheel
chapters.description: Augustine’s restlessness and the image of Maggie driving illustrate the move from striving to getting out of the way.

chapters.title: A map of unfolding
chapters.description: Matthew introduces liberative dependent origination as a causal map of freedom rather than suffering.

chapters.title: Faith to joy
chapters.description: Faith arises from suffering and hope, then matures into gladness and a more companionable path.

chapters.title: Rapture to tranquility
chapters.description: He describes pīti, embodied delight, and the way it settles into tranquility when striving relaxes.

chapters.title: Stillness and samadhi
chapters.description: The talk emphasizes confidence, quiet happiness, and samadhi as unification rather than forced concentration.

chapters.title: Disenchantment deepens
chapters.description: Matthew uses Ajahn Brahm and Ajahn Sucitto to show how aligned intention, less coping, and clearer seeing loosen craving.

title: Morning Instruction: Emotions
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a short morning instruction on working with emotions in meditation, using RAIN, breath, and sound to show how feeling can be met without turning it into a prediction about the future. He describes emotion as part of the body’s risk-detecting system, then gently shifts the practice toward opening to intensities, knots, longing, resistance, and dead zones without discrimination. The talk closes by returning to soothing supports and the basic inquiry, "Am I okay?"—not as a verdict on life, but as a practice experiment in direct experience.
short_summary: A quiet instruction on meeting emotion directly, without treating feeling as a forecast about the future.

chapters.title: RAIN and being held
chapters.description: He begins with RAIN and the feeling of being surrounded, held, and soothed by sound and breath.

chapters.title: Breath in the body
chapters.description: The instruction widens into the body, with rain, breath, and an inclusive awareness that can contain experience.

chapters.title: Emotion as signal
chapters.description: He explains emotion as part of how small creatures sense risk, reward, and the felt direction of life.

chapters.title: Not a forecast
chapters.description: Feeling is described as something we often interpret as a prediction, but practice asks us not to extrapolate into the future.

chapters.title: Opening to intensity
chapters.description: The teaching stays with urgency, knots, surges, longing, resistance, and the various textures of feeling without discrimination.

chapters.title: Am I okay?
chapters.description: He turns to the question "Am I okay?" and frames the Buddha's instruction as an experiment in how to answer it.

chapters.title: Soothed and sustained
chapters.description: The practice settles into repeated reminders of being soothed by sound, breath, love, and the lineage of practice while staying awake to shifting emotion.

chapters.title: An experiment only
chapters.description: He clarifies that this is not a rule for all of life, but a temporary experiment in treating intensities as the ground of practice.

title: Non-doing: Receptivity & Purification
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver contrasts two instincts on the Dharma path: the drive to manage, improve, and control experience, and the quieter discipline of receptivity. He traces how effort can become tangled with self-view, then turns toward openness, sensitivity, and learning as alternatives to willfulness. In the last part of the talk, he frames difficult phases of practice not as failures but as purification, where grief, pain, and love can all be part of the same release.
short_summary: A talk about moving from willful self-improvement toward receptivity, sensitivity, and purification.

chapters.title: Choice and choicelessness
chapters.description: Brensilver opens by contrasting wise choice with the less visible current of choiceless receptivity in practice.

chapters.title: Effort and willpower
chapters.description: He draws on Nietzsche and familiar practice rhetoric to show how effort can slide into force, self-rehabilitation, and spiritual striving.

chapters.title: The meditator program
chapters.description: Using Ajahn Sucitto, he names the meditator identity itself as another conditioned pattern shaped by self-criticism and control.

chapters.title: Receptivity as practice
chapters.description: He turns to openness, learning, and the idea that practice is not only about changing life but being changed by it.

chapters.title: Wanting shapes perception
chapters.description: The Mulapariyaya Sutta and reflections on perception show how desire and motivation color what seems to be simply seen.

chapters.title: Sensitivity and vulnerability
chapters.description: A Krishnamurti passage and Matthew's own reflections explore sensitivity, porousness, and relaxing the body's contractions.

chapters.title: Suffering and purification
chapters.description: He reframes the First Noble Truth through the difficult arcs of practice, where descent, helplessness, and grievance can become purification.

chapters.title: Grief, love, release
chapters.description: The talk closes with Shinzen Young and Leonard Cohen, tying purification to equanimity, grief, and a steadier love that survives loss.

title: Conversation about Insight
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores insight as something more than analysis: a shift that is freeing, embodied, and often difficult to distinguish from ordinary knowing until it has become real. He contrasts rumination with realization, then brings in examples from Rob Burbea, Nicole Krauss, Jack Kornfield, Ajahn Chah, and others to show how practice changes perception, attention, and the stories we live inside. The conversation later turns to samadhi, motivation, and the way craving and wanting shape what we think we know.
short_summary: A conversation about insight as embodied realization, and about how attention, samadhi, and reduced craving change the way experience is perceived.

chapters.title: Insight and Training
chapters.description: Matthew opens by questioning the idea that insight alone changes behavior, and reframes practice as a kind of training that works in more than one way.

chapters.title: What Insight Really Is
chapters.description: He develops Rob Burbea's view of insight as freeing, contrasts it with rumination, and describes insight as something that bubbles up rather than being forced.

chapters.title: Knowing and Realizing
chapters.description: The talk turns to the gap between knowing and making something real, with examples of dukkha, change, and the difference between propositional knowledge and practical wisdom.

chapters.title: New Feelings Enter
chapters.description: A long passage from Nicole Krauss is used to suggest that feeling itself can widen, and that insight may arrive like a new emotion or a new capacity for experience.

chapters.title: Enoughness and Not Knowing
chapters.description: Matthew argues that a sense of enoughness and a willingness to not know can broaden attention, loosen familiar interpretations, and make room for insight.

chapters.title: Samadhi and Consolidation
chapters.description: Drawing on Ajahn Chah and then a story about Jack Kornfield, he links insight with samadhi, embodiment, and the way insight becomes durable in the heart and body.

chapters.title: Accident-Prone Practice
chapters.description: In dialogue, he explains how to become more 'accident-prone' for insight by caring less about solving life and more about curiosity, openness, and loosened agendas.

chapters.title: Craving Shapes Perception
chapters.description: The conversation closes by stressing that wanting and knowing are intertwined, and that when craving softens the same experience can be seen differently, with a lighter relationship to narrative.

title: Whole Presence Samadhi
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver speaks about samadhi as more than concentration: a gathered, easeful, undistracted presence that can become non-separate, embodied, and whole. Using stories of a Zen teacher on a bus, a temple drum, Suzuki Roshi, and his own experience with striving in meditation, he shows how pressure and self-clinging can block the very stillness we want. The talk gradually shifts from defining samadhi to showing how kindness, acceptance, and working gently with hindrances make it available in ordinary life.
short_summary: A grounded exploration of samadhi as presence, non-separation, and wholeness, with stories that challenge striving.

chapters.title: Opening Samadhi
chapters.description: The talk begins with the plan for a shared Dharma conversation and a first attempt to define samadhi as gathered, easeful presence rather than effortful concentration.

chapters.title: Bus Ride Presence
chapters.description: A story about a calm older monk on a Kyoto bus shows how radiance and settled presence can quietly draw others in.

chapters.title: Undistracted Awareness
chapters.description: Matthew develops samadhi as continuous presence and distinguishes it from simple momentary mindfulness, using everyday absorption as an example.

chapters.title: Becoming One
chapters.description: The temple drum story illustrates non-separation, with samadhi described as becoming one with the action rather than standing apart from it.

chapters.title: Ordinary Acts
chapters.description: Suzuki Roshi is briefly invoked as an example of total presence in ordinary movement, and samadhi is linked to softness that can hold whatever arises.

chapters.title: Softness Through Suffering
chapters.description: A story from Crooked Cucumber reframes Suzuki Roshi’s spaciousness as something hard-won through tragedy, leading into samadhi as healing the divisions in the heart.

chapters.title: Striving and Craving
chapters.description: In the dialogue, Matthew reflects on how talk of bliss can create more suffering, and how chasing states or technique can become another form of clinging.

chapters.title: Hindrances and Wholeness
chapters.description: The conversation turns to the hindrances, embodied sensitivity, and the insight that samadhi is already present when resistance softens and the heart is allowed to be simple and open.

chapters.title: Closing Blessing
chapters.description: The talk closes with a short blessing and a final reminder that samadhi helps us see things as they are, meeting the moment with patience and kindness.

title: Guided Meditation: Self and Space
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet guided meditation built around non-striving, resting in awareness, and easing the habit of making something out of the moment. He gradually moves from the simplicity of being alive, through repeated rests and reflections on self-making, toward a practice of leaning into what does not depend on the self. The talk closes by widening this into a daily practice of sincerity and asking, moment by moment, what opens space now.
short_summary: A guided meditation on resting in awareness, loosening selfing, and finding space without striving.

chapters.title: Simple aliveness
chapters.description: The meditation opens with a Suzuki Roshi attribution and an invitation to stop extracting anything from the moment.

chapters.title: Giving up acquisition
chapters.description: Matthew contrasts generosity with acquisitiveness and suggests that movement in practice unfolds when the need to get somewhere is relaxed.

chapters.title: Rest where awareness stays
chapters.description: He introduces the instruction to rest in what cannot be stopped and repeats it as a sustained practice cue.

chapters.title: Putting down life
chapters.description: A poetic image of the 'rag and bone shop of our heart' leads into the instruction to put down the notion of my life and rest.

chapters.title: Hindrances and disenchantment
chapters.description: Hindrances are framed as innocent but unworkable, and the result is a sober kind of disenchantment.

chapters.title: How self is reiterated
chapters.description: Matthew describes selfing as a kind of proprioception built from reference points like body, preferences, and life story.

chapters.title: Rest in dependence
chapters.description: He turns toward resting in what does not depend on the self, using that as a bridge into deeper openness.

chapters.title: Love and surrender
chapters.description: The meditation settles into prolonged silence and repeated 'okay,' pointing to the tenderness needed to make this bearable.

chapters.title: Practice in daily life
chapters.description: In the closing section, Matthew reframes meditation as an exploration of freedom, tailored to the heart in the present moment.

title: Restlessness, Worry & Compassion
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver uses the ordinary drama of making oatmeal to open a wider reflection on restlessness, worry, and the habit of trying to control the future. He moves from Buddhist ideas about anicca, dukkha, and bhava tanha into the practical work of settling the mind, meeting uncertainty, and recognizing many alarm bells as false alarms. The talk gradually turns toward compassion: for grief, for regret, and for the fact that freedom asks for love rather than perfection.
short_summary: A quiet Dharma talk on how worry tries to manage uncertainty, and how compassion helps us stay with impermanence.

chapters.title: Oatmeal and worry
chapters.description: Brensilver begins with a comic meditation on oatmeal as a window into his own restless mind.

chapters.title: Restlessness as the preface
chapters.description: He names restlessness and worry as the true topic and argues that practice often means stopping what we keep adding to the moment.

chapters.title: Freud and uncertainty
chapters.description: Using Freud as a bridge, he describes anxiety as a clue to mental life and restlessness as forward-leaning energy tied to samsara and incompletion.

chapters.title: Worry as control
chapters.description: He explores fear, prediction, and the mind’s attempt to govern uncertainty, linking this to the craving to become and the settling power of tranquility.

chapters.title: Roots, safety, and false alarms
chapters.description: With Ajahn Sucitto and a pain analogy, he shifts from getting things right to becoming rooted, distinguishing real danger from the mind’s false alarms.

chapters.title: Present-moment practice
chapters.description: A cited scientist’s account of meditation leads into his picture of practice as pruning counterfactual thinking and learning to stay with the present.

chapters.title: Compassion for groundlessness
chapters.description: Drawing on Pema Chödrön, he says impermanence has to be grieved before it becomes refuge, and that compassion is what lets the heart soften.

chapters.title: Regret and forgiveness
chapters.description: He turns to regret, accountability, and forgiveness, showing how to meet harm without building a heavier self-story around it.

chapters.title: The broken cup
chapters.description: He closes with Ajahn Chah’s broken cup teaching, using impermanence to clarify gratitude, love, and how we choose to spend what remains.

title: Guided Meditation: Affect and Motivation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that begins with posture, breath, and a felt sense of the body as a constellation of sensations rather than a fixed outline. He then turns toward affect and motivation, encouraging close attention to the body’s signals of wanting, aversion, and urgency without preferring spaciousness over solidification. In the later teaching, he links clinging to these affective circuits, points to how narratives try to explain experience, and returns to the value of staying with practice, equanimity, and the pace of awareness.
short_summary: A guided meditation on posture, breath, body sensation, and the bodily roots of affect, motivation, and clinging.

chapters.title: Settling into posture
chapters.description: The talk opens with a brief orientation to upright, grounded presence, balancing sky and earth.

chapters.title: Breath and relaxation
chapters.description: He invites a few breaths that support relaxation and begin to soften the body.

chapters.title: Body as sensation
chapters.description: Attention shifts from body image to the changing field of sensations, with the body felt as porous and spacious.

chapters.title: Softening the heart
chapters.description: The repeated invitation to let the breath reach the heart deepens the sense of openness and pervasiveness.

chapters.title: No preference for spaciousness
chapters.description: He emphasizes that mindfulness and love should dissolve solidity without clinging to openness as a better state.

chapters.title: Affect and motivation
chapters.description: The teaching moves into urges, pressure, liking and disliking, showing how emotional movement arises in the body.

chapters.title: Knowing and letting go
chapters.description: He explores how knowing is suffused by wanting, and repeatedly returns to gentle, non-grasping awareness.

chapters.title: Clinging and narrative
chapters.description: Clinging is linked to affective circuits, and he describes how stories about self and life grow from bodily feeling.

chapters.title: Practice rhythms and closing
chapters.description: The talk ends with guidance to stay close, work at the pace of awareness, meet boredom with equanimity, and then transitions into retreat announcements.

title: Ill-Will & Metta
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores ill-will and metta as two very different ways the heart meets suffering. He moves from the mechanics of aversion—how craving, blame, and anger narrow experience—to the possibility of purification, radical accountability, and a softer, more spacious response. Near the end, he turns toward loving-kindness as a form of non-violence that can hold pain without collapse, and he closes by linking wisdom with love and non-contention.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on how aversion narrows experience, and how metta opens a more honest and compassionate way to meet suffering.

chapters.title: Opening the problem
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces ill-will and frames it as a familiar way of suffering that the talk will examine closely.

chapters.title: The Dharma as a broad remedy
chapters.description: He uses pharmacology to argue that Dharma works through many mechanisms, not just the surface sign of a calm mind.

chapters.title: Purification and practice
chapters.description: He distinguishes concentration from purification and says the path includes peaks, valleys, and whatever is present now.

chapters.title: What and how
chapters.description: The talk turns to the 'what' and the deeper 'how' of practice, showing how motivations shape what we seem to know.

chapters.title: Craving shapes the view
chapters.description: He explores how craving and aversion thicken experience, using his own childhood sensitivity to show how ill-will can become a strategy for managing pain.

chapters.title: Mara and the turning inward
chapters.description: Brensilver describes the habitual impulses to approach, avoid, or ignore, then shows how clinging and aversion reinforce helplessness and suffering.

chapters.title: The heat of aversion
chapters.description: Using the boiling-pot simile, he explains how ill-will obscures judgment, while Agnes Callard, Ruth King, and the Dalai Lama help refine the view of anger as partially functional but never transformative.

chapters.title: Facing and owning anger
chapters.description: He turns to noticing ill-will directly, describing radical accountability as a training that stops externalizing blame without denying external causes.

chapters.title: Metta as antidote
chapters.description: The emphasis shifts to metta as warm friendliness, free of possessiveness and control, and as a way of being moved by goodness and finding a home in it.

title: Guided Meditation: Attention & Awareness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a guided meditation that moves from settling into practice to a close look at attention, clinging, and the ease that comes from making peace with the present moment. He distinguishes the “spotlight” of attention from awareness itself, then gradually shifts toward resting in the knowing that cannot be turned off. The talk is quiet and reflective, with extended meditation space and a final emphasis on relinquishing control.
short_summary: A guided meditation on softening attention, easing clinging, and resting in awareness.

chapters.title: Arriving to meditate
chapters.description: The session begins by noticing the transition from ordinary conversation into formal practice and allowing that shift to be simple.

chapters.title: Natural beginning
chapters.description: The meditation starts with a natural, unforced attitude, trusting that wisdom and love are already present.

chapters.title: Relaxing and letting go
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward relaxing what can be relaxed and releasing some of the agitation held in attention.

chapters.title: Attention settles
chapters.description: As peace with the imperfection of the moment grows, the spotlight of attention becomes steadier and more fluid.

chapters.title: Less control
chapters.description: The practice deepens by relinquishing the need to direct attention so tightly.

chapters.title: Resting in awareness
chapters.description: The focus shifts from the objects of attention to awareness itself, which cannot really be stopped.

chapters.title: Spotlight and clinging
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on how attention can support concentration, but also how it can serve clinging when it is turned into a self.

chapters.title: Unpartitioned knowing
chapters.description: The meditation opens into the sense that knowing is already happening, not owned by a separate self, and that awareness no longer tastes like 'me'.

title: Dharmette: The Pleasure of Spiritual Friendship
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on the pleasure of spiritual friendship as a quiet but important part of Dharma life. He describes intimacy as the willingness to see and be seen, and argues that good friends help us be known in both our strength and our imperfection. The talk moves from the role of Sangha to the way friendship reorients attention toward goodness, beauty, and the urgency of practice.
short_summary: A brief Dharma talk on how spiritual friendship helps us see, be seen, and remember goodness.

chapters.title: Opening the Theme
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the talk as part of a series on Dharma pleasures and names friendship as the focus for this dharmette.

chapters.title: Seeing and Being Seen
chapters.description: He uses a colleague’s definition of intimacy to frame friendship as mutual visibility, including being known at both our best and worst.

chapters.title: Known in Imperfection
chapters.description: The talk turns to primary relationships and the need to be seen clearly, both lovingly and honestly, without hiding our flaws or our strengths.

chapters.title: Sangha and Goodness
chapters.description: Matthew describes Sangha as a community of spiritual friends whose attention is directed toward goodness, and he quotes Kinnell on reteaching things their loveliness.

chapters.title: Shaping the Mind
chapters.description: He reflects on how porous and suggestible we are, and how our surroundings quietly train our views toward wealth, power, wisdom, or love.

chapters.title: Reminders of Dharma
chapters.description: Using a road trip through California as an example, he suggests we need conditions and friends that keep reconnecting us to wisdom and the urgency of practice.

chapters.title: Connoisseurs of Goodness
chapters.description: Matthew compares practice to developing an eye for art: we become more sensitive to beauty in others and more able to revere the same things together.

chapters.title: Closing Blessing
chapters.description: He ends by speaking about teaching as mutual recognition, thanking listeners and volunteers, and resting the talk in gratitude and goodwill.

title: Guided Meditation: Sound, Outer and Inner
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation on listening, beginning with settling and sensing goodness, then opening attention to the outer soundscape. The practice gradually turns toward sound as passing phenomenon, including the mind's own verbal thought, so that thinking can be heard more as another sound than as self. The talk stays close to direct experience and repeatedly returns to letting sounds move through without grasping.
short_summary: A guided meditation that moves from settling into sound awareness to hearing thoughts as part of the same passing soundscape.

chapters.title: Opening and Settling
chapters.description: After brief house-keeping, the practice begins by settling into the body, the room, and a sense of what is good in the moment.

chapters.title: Beginning with Goodness
chapters.description: Matthew invites an easeful start, encouraging sensitivity to intention, the sangha, and the goodness already present in practice.

chapters.title: Outer Soundscape
chapters.description: The meditation turns to ambient sound, with the invitation to notice near and distant sounds, beginnings and endings, and the open space in which they appear.

chapters.title: Sound Passing Through
chapters.description: He uses the image of sound like wind moving through the body and mind, contrasting this with the habit of grasping, liking, and disliking.

chapters.title: Hearing Inner Speech
chapters.description: Attention shifts inward as verbal thought is treated as auditory phenomenon that awareness can notice and interrupt.

chapters.title: Thoughts as Sound
chapters.description: The teaching is restated plainly: thoughts are not a special category, but belong with birds, trucks, and neighbors in the same field of listening.

title: Guided Meditation: Faith in Awareness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on trust in awareness rather than trying to engineer experience. The practice moves from settling posture and sensing what is present, to recognizing that even ordinary stillness is both familiar and fresh, to relaxing the search for signs of progress. He closes by emphasizing that the seeds of awakening are already here and that the practitioner’s work can become simpler: listen carefully, attune, and rest.
short_summary: A guided meditation on trusting awareness, releasing the need for signs of progress, and resting in the already-present conditions for awakening.

chapters.title: Entering the sit
chapters.description: Brensilver welcomes the group, frames the morning session as an exploration of technique, and invites everyone to settle into posture.

chapters.title: Familiar and fresh
chapters.description: He turns attention toward the immediate feel of sitting, noticing how stillness and silence are both well known and newly alive.

chapters.title: Meeting this moment
chapters.description: The instruction opens into curiosity about what this day brings, including whatever insight may be latent in the present body and mind.

chapters.title: Dharma posture
chapters.description: He describes a posture of awareness and heart that can be cultivated no matter what phenomena arise, serving long-term welfare and happiness.

chapters.title: No need for signs
chapters.description: The talk addresses the habit of looking for proof of progress and suggests that healing and awakening often unfold in ways we cannot directly see.

chapters.title: Faith and trust
chapters.description: Brensilver emphasizes settling back into faith in awareness and trusting that the Dharma is working through us, simplifying the practitioner’s role.

chapters.title: Already enough
chapters.description: He underscores the relief of not having to engineer the moment and points to the seeds of awakening as already present.

chapters.title: Listen and rest
chapters.description: The meditation closes by asking for careful listening, attunement to wisdom and compassion, and a final resting back into the practice.

title: Dharmette: The Pleasure of Purification
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on why the difficult parts of practice matter, arguing that the path is not only about peaks of calm or insight but also about the descent, the valley, and the discomfort that can carry the taste of purification. He contrasts the partial pleasure of clinging with the quieter relief that comes from meeting pain with equanimity, surrender, and a willingness to be changed. The talk draws on Ajahn Chah and Shinzen Young to show how wholesome discomfort can become a kind of joy, softness, and freedom.
short_summary: A short talk on how the discomfort of practice can become a pleasurable taste of purification rather than just suffering.

chapters.title: Beyond the Peaks
chapters.description: Brensilver opens by questioning the idea that practice is mainly about spiritual highs and instead includes ascent, descent, and valley alike.

chapters.title: Two Tastes
chapters.description: He names the central distinction of the talk: the valley of practice can taste like suffering or like purification.

chapters.title: Acquired Taste
chapters.description: Using coffee as a metaphor, he describes purification as an acquired taste and the challenge of learning to value wholesome discomfort.

chapters.title: Clinging's Reward
chapters.description: He explains how clinging partly delivers pleasure, making it hard to release, and why practice means moving against those reinforcing cycles.

chapters.title: Pain of Healing
chapters.description: A physical therapy story illustrates the difference between pain that injures and pain that heals, offering relief as part of purification.

chapters.title: Deep Joy
chapters.description: Quoting Shinzen Young, he describes purification as a kind of joy in which blockages are worked through and separation loosens.

chapters.title: Surrender and Exposure
chapters.description: He turns to impermanence, bracing, and surrender, suggesting that purification works like exposure therapy when we stop defending against experience.

chapters.title: Freedom Afterward
chapters.description: Brensilver notes that after choosing this difficult path, clinging and hatred become less compelling and less effortful to abandon.

chapters.title: Poignant Love
chapters.description: He closes by describing the human condition as more poignant, with a strange but real pleasure, and returns to the contrast between suffering and purification.

title: Guided Meditation: Unseduced by the Mirage of the Future
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a gentle guided meditation centered on loosening the habit of performing in practice and meeting experience without trying to prove anything. The meditation moves from settling the body and attention, to relaxing self-conscious effort, to seeing how the future can seduce the mind into anxiety and projection. He returns again and again to simple phrases of enoughness, encouraging a willingness to rest in what is already present.
short_summary: A guided meditation on relaxing self-conscious striving and meeting the present without being seduced by imagined futures.

chapters.title: Opening and settling
chapters.description: The meditation opens with a brief welcome and a soft invitation to begin sitting simply and naturally.

chapters.title: Natural beginning
chapters.description: He asks listeners to notice what they think meditation is supposed to look like and to loosen that inherited script.

chapters.title: No need to prove
chapters.description: Matthew names the urge to perform for oneself, a teacher, or even the Buddha, and gently releases that pressure.

chapters.title: Attending lightly
chapters.description: He suggests using breath, body, sound, and closed-eye darkness as anchors while avoiding a brittle, achievement-oriented attention.

chapters.title: Time and projection
chapters.description: The practice turns toward how we imagine trajectories, outcomes, and identities, and how that can sandwich us inside time.

chapters.title: Unseduced by future
chapters.description: He directly names the mirage of the future and points to the gap between wanting and actually arriving.

chapters.title: Enoughness and surrender
chapters.description: The meditation settles into phrases of safety and sufficiency, with a quiet surrender into what is here.

chapters.title: Opening to phenomena
chapters.description: He closes by encouraging a steady openness to pleasant and unpleasant experience without spinning outward, resting back in awareness as the bell rings.

title: Dharmette: Sourcing our Motivation from Love Rather than Fear
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on the difference between motivating ourselves through fear and motivating ourselves through love. Using a story from residential treatment work, he shows how harsh self-management can seem effective at first, but tends not to support lasting change. The talk moves toward a quieter alternative: curiosity, humor, tenderness, and care as the basis for practice and daily life.
short_summary: A short talk on shifting motivation from self-harshness and fear toward love, care, and a lighter relationship to practice.

chapters.title: A Different Dharma Pleasure
chapters.description: He introduces the theme of finding pleasure in practice, especially the pleasure of sourcing motivation from love.

chapters.title: Working With Boys
chapters.description: A story from residential treatment illustrates how behavior programs can be driven by either fear or care.

chapters.title: Fear or Love
chapters.description: He names the tension between short-term compliance and the kind of change that can actually last.

chapters.title: What Drives Us
chapters.description: The talk turns inward, asking what kind of program we run on ourselves and where our motivation comes from.

chapters.title: Self-Harshness and Proof
chapters.description: He describes self-hatred, punishment, and gold-star thinking as ways of trying to change through fear and proving.

chapters.title: Curiosity and Humor
chapters.description: As shame loosens, limitations can be held with curiosity and even laughter rather than judgment.

chapters.title: Care Over Urgency
chapters.description: He points to tenderness, sympathy, and a sober appreciation of our condition as a steadier basis for growth.

chapters.title: Less Entangled
chapters.description: The close emphasizes lightness, discovery, and a motivation rooted in love rather than fear.

title: Guided Meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet guided meditation centered on letting attention settle into soothing phenomena and the silence behind experience. The practice moves from posture and settling, to finding ease in the body and environment, to resting with whatever arises without needing to fix anything. He closes by reframing practice as a simple Dharma gesture: let time pass, let the teachings unfold, and trust the wholesomeness of intention.
short_summary: A gentle guided meditation that emphasizes ease, silence, and resting in wholesome intention.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins with a brief welcome and a direction to find a sustainable posture and settle the body.

chapters.title: Letting Practice Lead
chapters.description: The instruction turns toward allowing Dharma, habit, and prior practice to guide the body rather than the thinking mind.

chapters.title: Opening to Stillness
chapters.description: Attention is invited to quiet, silence, and the felt sense of being here as the meditation deepens.

chapters.title: Finding Soothing Objects
chapters.description: He suggests noticing phenomena that bring ease, such as the breath, bodily sensations, or the darkness behind closed eyes.

chapters.title: Resting In Ease
chapters.description: The practice continues in silence with repeated reminders to let the mind take cues from calming objects of attention.

chapters.title: Tranquility and Healing
chapters.description: He names tranquility as healing and as a way for the heart's unfinished places and transient aches to be held in awareness.

chapters.title: Goodness of Intention
chapters.description: The teaching widens to include whatever is arising, along with the reassurance that the intention to wake up is itself soothing and good.

chapters.title: Time As Ally
chapters.description: Near the end, he emphasizes that practice is not a project, but a way of letting time and awareness do their work.

title: Dharma Pleasure - The Pleasure of Having a Path
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores the "pleasure of having a path" as a kind of Dharma pleasure that does not feed craving. He begins with the idea that ordinary pleasures often intensify thirst, then turns to the relief that comes from finding a practice that feels personally trustworthy. The talk moves through faith, purpose, and direction, using brief references to Nietzsche, Vaclav Havel, and Ken McLeod to show how confidence in wisdom and love can steady practice even when life remains unclear.
short_summary: A quiet reflection on how faith in a trustworthy path can itself be a form of relief and pleasure.

chapters.title: Opening the theme
chapters.description: Matthew greets the group and introduces the week's theme of Dharma pleasures, especially the idea that some pleasures leave peace rather than more thirst.

chapters.title: Pleasure and craving
chapters.description: He distinguishes Dharma pleasure from ordinary pleasure, describing how ordinary pleasure can reinforce craving while Dharma pleasure satiates.

chapters.title: Finding a path
chapters.description: He turns to the relief of discovering a path that fits, recounting his own skepticism and the sense of ease that came with finding a workable way.

chapters.title: Having a why
chapters.description: A Nietzsche quote frames the teaching that a convincing purpose changes how difficulty is borne and makes the rest of practice feel secondary.

chapters.title: Faith without certainty
chapters.description: Using Vaclav Havel, he describes hope and faith as confidence that something makes sense, without needing guaranteed outcomes or constant proof.

chapters.title: Direction in confusion
chapters.description: A longer quote attributed to Ken McLeod shows how resting in confusion can still give rise to clarity, direction, and trust in the path.

chapters.title: Closing reflection
chapters.description: He sums up the practical solace of having direction and leaves the talk as an invitation to consider that confidence for oneself.

title: Seven Factors of Awakening
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on the seven factors of awakening as a living structure of practice rather than a dry list. He moves from mindfulness and investigation through energy, rapture, tranquility, samadhi, and equanimity, showing how the factors support one another and mature over time. The talk emphasizes that awakening is both cultivated and discovered: the path and the fruit are not separate.
short_summary: A quiet, practical exploration of the seven factors of awakening as both path and fruit, with attention to balance, curiosity, effort, and equanimity.

chapters.title: Why This List Matters
chapters.description: Brensilver opens by revisiting why Buddhist lists can become invisible through familiarity and why they still matter deeply in practice.

chapters.title: Path and Fruit
chapters.description: He frames the Dharma as release from suffering and introduces the seven factors of awakening as the active wholesome qualities that support that release.

chapters.title: A Teaching in Sickness
chapters.description: A sutta story of the Buddha asking for the factors at night leads into the idea that these qualities are practical support, not abstract doctrine.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and Balance
chapters.description: Using Tejaniya’s causal framing, he describes the interplay between the energizing and tranquilizing sides of practice and rethinks what mindfulness means.

chapters.title: What Mindfulness Does
chapters.description: He argues that mindfulness is a broad umbrella term, then narrows it as awareness that sees suffering without being compelled by it and gradually becomes a habit.

chapters.title: Curiosity and Investigation
chapters.description: Investigation is presented as sustained, curious attention rather than rumination, with faith growing through careful looking and repeated inquiry.

chapters.title: Energy and Rapture
chapters.description: The talk turns to effort as necessary resistance against habitual suffering, then to rapture as the surprising, pleasurable vitality that can arise from letting go.

chapters.title: Tranquility and Samadhi
chapters.description: Brensilver describes the pivot into calming the nervous system, where steadiness, unification, and a more durable realization of Dharma become possible.

chapters.title: Equanimity and Release
chapters.description: He closes with equanimity as a softening of clinging, a quiet nearness to awakening, and a place where peace and love are not far apart.

title: Guided Meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that moves from settling the body into a spacious way of meeting sound, sensation, and emotion. The talk gently reframes bracing and aversion as habits of protection, then invites a more trusting openness in which experience can arise without being organized around a defended center. In the second half, the practice softens toward a sustained sense of awareness as something that can hold and mellow the forces of suffering.
short_summary: A quiet guided meditation on settling, allowing experience, and softening through open awareness.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation opens with orientation, a comfortable posture, and a gradual settling into the room and the body.

chapters.title: Welcoming Experience
chapters.description: He invites a welcoming mind toward sounds, bodily intensity, and the ordinary feeling of arriving.

chapters.title: Breath and Ease
chapters.description: The guidance turns to the breath as a gentle way to smooth the energy of body and mind.

chapters.title: Letting Winds Blow
chapters.description: Bracing, self-protection, and the winds of sensation, thought, and affect are introduced as experiences to allow rather than resist.

chapters.title: Grasping and Aversion
chapters.description: He extends the teaching to unpleasantness and the pull of grasping and aversion, emphasizing openness instead of reaction.

chapters.title: No Need To Pretend
chapters.description: The talk states plainly that practice does not ask us to fake neutrality or force liking, only to meet experience clearly.

chapters.title: Softening The Heart
chapters.description: A more affective phase emerges, with the heart being softened by greed and aversion held within awareness.

chapters.title: Sustained Open Awareness
chapters.description: The closing stretch remains with the same simple refrain, letting awareness do the work of holding and softening experience.

title: Guided Meditation: Effort and Effortlessness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation moves from settling the body into a simple, lightly held practice of attention. Matthew Brensilver emphasizes the difference between effortful attention and the more effortless quality of awareness, using the breath or another anchor as a relaxed point of return rather than something to force. Later, he invites a softer relationship to experience, including a memorable instruction to let the face fall off as a way of loosening identification and resting in openness.
short_summary: A guided meditation on using gentle effort to steady attention while resting more and more in effortless awareness.

chapters.title: Landing and settling
chapters.description: The practice begins by arriving in posture and allowing the mind to land without trying to predict the session.

chapters.title: Choosing an anchor
chapters.description: Attention is directed to an object like the breath, while the rest of experience is allowed to remain in view.

chapters.title: Effort and awareness
chapters.description: Matthew draws a distinction between the effort of attention training and the quieter, seemingly effortless quality of awareness.

chapters.title: Relaxing the grip
chapters.description: The instruction shifts toward easing control and recognizing the space in which experiences arise.

chapters.title: Less mesmerized
chapters.description: As discursive thinking loosens, awareness feels less like a personal possession and more like open knowing.

chapters.title: Letting the face soften
chapters.description: A cited instruction from Pandit becomes a teaching on relaxation and non-identification with the face and body.

chapters.title: Opening further
chapters.description: The meditation continues with repeated invitations to let bodily self-image drop into the openness of awareness.

title: Dharmette: Dharma as Existential Therapy
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a kind of existential therapy: a way of meeting death, impermanence, freedom, isolation, and responsibility without flinching. He moves from stark quotations about mortality into a quiet case for practicing with groundlessness, building meaning from within lived experience, and becoming more ethically responsive to others. The talk stays practical and grounded, with literary and Buddhist references used to clarify the work of waking up.
short_summary: A grounded talk on how Dharma helps us face mortality, make meaning, and live responsibly in an uncertain world.

chapters.title: Facing Mortality
chapters.description: Matthew opens with Russell and a Zen-style reminder to put mortality and urgency at the center of practice.

chapters.title: Dharma as Therapy
chapters.description: He names Dharma as attention training, cognitive therapy, exposure therapy, and finally existential therapy.

chapters.title: Learning Groundlessness
chapters.description: He describes anicca as something the heart acclimatizes to through bearable doses of impermanence and loss.

chapters.title: Meaning Is Made
chapters.description: Using Yalom and the Buddha, he argues that the world is contingent and that meaning has to be built rather than found.

chapters.title: Building a Life
chapters.description: He turns to Philip Appleman and Leonard Cohen to suggest that practice helps us get straight with ourselves and spend our freedom well.

chapters.title: Alone, Yet Responsible
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on aloneness, then broadens the question to what we owe others across distance and time.

chapters.title: Do Better
chapters.description: He closes with Maya Angelou’s ethic of responding as we learn, followed by brief thanks and farewells.

title: Guided Meditation: Getting Somewhere by Going Nowhere
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores the paradox of meditating with aspiration while letting go of striving. He starts by contrasting goal-directed effort with a quieter, atelic way of practicing, then moves into a guided opening that settles attention into the body and softens grasping. From there the talk widens into a contemplation of awareness, liberation, and the way practice privileges presence over the relentless need to get somewhere.
short_summary: A guided meditation on practicing with aspiration but without urgency, moving from body awareness into a relaxed, liberating view of experience.

chapters.title: Goals and Dukkha
chapters.description: Matthew begins by examining the problem of goals: they give meaning, but they also carry unsatisfactoriness because wanting and getting are never the same.

chapters.title: Telic and Atelic
chapters.description: He introduces the distinction between goal-directed and non-goal-directed activity and uses it to frame meditation as both aspirational and non-striving.

chapters.title: One Moment at a Time
chapters.description: The talk turns toward practice as a steady cultivation of qualities like samadhi, done moment by moment without grasping or engineering.

chapters.title: Aspiration Without Urgency
chapters.description: Matthew names the deepest aims of practice as wisdom, compassion, understanding, and love, and encourages reverence for those qualities without pushing to get anywhere.

chapters.title: Settling Into the Body
chapters.description: The guided meditation opens into body awareness, inviting a full, intimate attention to sensation and a softening into the present field.

chapters.title: Liberation in Phenomena
chapters.description: He brings in the Buddha's teaching that all phenomena have liberation as their core, contrasting this with how grasping makes experience feel solid.

chapters.title: Relaxing the Struggle
chapters.description: The practice continues by releasing clinging, tension, and the urge to get organized, shifting toward a simpler willingness to be aware.

chapters.title: Be Blissful
chapters.description: The closing stretches into repeated encouragement to rest in bliss and let awareness come before the struggle to manage life.

title: Dharmette: Dharma as Exposure Therapy for Samsara
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a kind of exposure therapy: turning toward the thoughts, feelings, and bodily states we usually avoid. He moves from clinical language about experiential avoidance to the way meditation, especially retreat practice, gradually reveals dukkha and asks for a deeper intimacy with it. The talk closes with a few reflective references that sharpen the point that what is bitter can become nourishment when it is fully met.
short_summary: A short Dharma talk connecting meditation, purification, and exposure therapy as a way of meeting the parts of experience we usually avoid.

chapters.title: Three ways of framing Dharma
chapters.description: He briefly links earlier talks on attention therapy and cognitive therapy before introducing today’s frame: Dharma as exposure therapy.

chapters.title: Avoidance and suffering
chapters.description: Brensilver defines experiential avoidance, names it as a trans-diagnostic problem, and argues that many psychological problems are also bad solutions.

chapters.title: What exposure means
chapters.description: He spells out how exposure therapy works in clinical settings, including approaching feared experiences step by step and building new learning around safety.

chapters.title: Dharma and samsara
chapters.description: The clinical example becomes a Dharma claim: practice is unsystematic exposure therapy for samsara, where anything that can disrupt peace eventually will.

chapters.title: Purification through practice
chapters.description: He describes relinquishing familiar ways of soothing and neutralizing distress, and names the difficult but wholesome quality of purification.

chapters.title: Taste for purification
chapters.description: A reference to Shinzen Young leads into a meditation survey and then into the idea that stronger concentration often reveals more dukkha rather than less.

chapters.title: Meeting primal forces
chapters.description: He describes retreat as a move from relative peace into purification, where rage, terror, helplessness, and conditioning are repeatedly brought into relationship.

chapters.title: Nourishment from bitterness
chapters.description: A brief quoted journal passage and a final reflection bring the talk to a close, suggesting that what seems bitter can become knowledge once it is fully eaten and transformed.

title: Guided Meditation: Meditation as an Atelic Activity
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver begins with a philosopher’s critique of goal-directed living and connects it to the Buddha’s teaching on dukkha: the strain of always completing one thing in order to chase the next. He then turns that frame toward meditation, emphasizing that practice is not only a tool for improvement but also a chance to step out of striving and rest in present-moment experience. The guided portion keeps returning to the same point: the breath, the body, and even thinking itself are reminders to loosen achievement and let this moment be enough.
short_summary: A guided meditation on loosening goal-seeking and resting in the present as an atelic, non-achievement practice.

chapters.title: Goals and Their Limits
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces Kieran Setiya’s argument that goal-centered life can become self-defeating because completed goals stop guiding us.

chapters.title: Dukkha and Fatigue
chapters.description: He connects that idea to the Buddha’s teaching on dukkha, naming the tiredness of continually replacing one completed goal with another.

chapters.title: Meditation Beyond Goals
chapters.description: The talk shifts to meditation as both goal-directed and not goal-directed, with atelic practice defined as simply being present with nowhere to go.

chapters.title: Arriving in the Body
chapters.description: Listeners are invited to settle posture, arrive through the breath, and gently put down the impulse to achieve anything.

chapters.title: Settling and Surrendering
chapters.description: In the extended guided period, he repeatedly releases progress, concentration, insight, and even the wish to get somewhere else.

chapters.title: When Striving Returns
chapters.description: He names how deeply habit can turn meditation into a self-improvement project, and invites surrender of all ideas of progress or regress.

chapters.title: Breath as Reminder
chapters.description: The anchor of breath, body, or sound is presented not as something to master but as a cue to relax striving and stay here.

chapters.title: Thoughts Beside the Point
chapters.description: He notes that thinking can be useful for planning and solving problems, but in practice it can quietly reintroduce goal-making and should be gently set aside.

chapters.title: Rest in Imperfection
chapters.description: The closing settles on the refuge of this moment’s imperfection, where striving and self-evaluation can finally rest.

title: Dharmette: Dharma as Cognitive Therapy
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a form of cognitive therapy: not just training attention, but changing the views and assumptions that organize experience. He moves from Aaron Beck’s model of schemas to Buddhist wise view, the three characteristics, and a fresh way of seeing the self. The talk ends with a practical note that attentional steadiness helps these new views take deeper root.
short_summary: A clear comparison of cognitive therapy and Dharma, centered on how wise view reshapes suffering, selfing, and choice.

chapters.title: Opening the frame
chapters.description: Matthew begins by situating the talk and naming that he wants to expand the earlier discussion beyond attention alone.

chapters.title: More than attention
chapters.description: He argues that mindfulness works in part because it changes more than attention, and that religion can be understood as a kind of cognitive therapy.

chapters.title: Beck’s schemas
chapters.description: Using Aaron Beck’s language, he explains how schemas, distorted appraisal, and new information can shift symptoms and behavior.

chapters.title: Wise view
chapters.description: He turns to Buddhist practice as cognitive therapy, emphasizing that wise view comes first and shapes the rest of the path.

chapters.title: A fresh self
chapters.description: Matthew introduces a different view of self, including Gilbert Ryle’s category mistake example, to loosen fixed identity.

chapters.title: Seeing our stories
chapters.description: He describes practice as noticing the stories and hidden beliefs we live by, including moralistic assumptions about happiness and worth.

chapters.title: Three characteristics
chapters.description: The talk gathers around dukkha, anicca, and anatta as corrective lenses for common fantasies about pleasure, control, and completion.

chapters.title: Attention and carryover
chapters.description: He closes by linking cognitive change with attentional steadiness, suggesting that concentration helps new views settle more deeply.

title: Guided Meditation: No Fidelity to the Past Moment
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet guided meditation built around the phrase “no fidelity to the past moment,” beginning with a short reading from Catherine Schultz about the mixed and changing nature of emotion. He then turns that passage into a Dharma point: practice does not try to preserve a mood or force a state to continue, and attention can stay open to both continuity and discontinuity. The sitting unfolds into instructions about landing in the body, relaxing the urge to narrate experience, and meeting whatever comes next with freshness and intimacy.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting experience without clinging to the mood or story of the previous moment.

chapters.title: Opening Welcome
chapters.description: Matthew greets the group and prepares to begin the meditation with a short reading.

chapters.title: Reading on Mixed Feeling
chapters.description: He reads from Catherine Schultz on how grief, gratitude, anger, boredom, and happiness mingle rather than appearing one at a time.

chapters.title: No Fidelity to the Past
chapters.description: The reading becomes a Dharma reflection on not carrying forward the last moment or trying to engineer the next one.

chapters.title: Settling Into Practice
chapters.description: After the reading, he guides listeners to let words melt away, land in the body, and come back to intimacy with the present.

chapters.title: Listening With Spaciousness
chapters.description: The instruction deepens into listening to oneself with an open heart, allowing moods to persist without assuming they must stay.

chapters.title: Freshness and Discontinuity
chapters.description: He emphasizes not narrating meditation into a fixed story and staying open to the morphing of sensory experience and insight.

chapters.title: Moment-by-Moment Offering
chapters.description: The close returns to freshness, questioning what comes next, and resting in a simple offering of attention to the moment.

title: Dharmette: Dharma as an Attention Therapy
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as a kind of training for attention: a way of recovering agency, reducing fragmentation, and making insight more durable. He moves from the pressures modern life places on attention, through examples of attentional disturbance in depression, anxiety, addiction, and ordinary suffering, to the steadiness of samadhi and how it helps Dharma lessons sink in more deeply. The talk ends by emphasizing that concentration and insight belong together, and that deeper happiness is tied to open-heartedness rather than closed-hearted control.
short_summary: A short Dharma talk on how meditation trains attention, steadies the mind, and helps insight take root.

chapters.title: Opening the framework
chapters.description: Matthew introduces a framework for how Dharma practice works on us, then names attention as the first mechanism he wants to explore.

chapters.title: Attention as agency
chapters.description: He argues that attention is tied closely to volition and selfhood, making its training central to practice and to a sense of agency.

chapters.title: Modern life and mind
chapters.description: He describes how technology and modern life fragment attention, drawing on William James and Ajahn Chah to underscore how precious and vulnerable attention is.

chapters.title: Disturbed attention
chapters.description: He connects fragmented attention with depression, trauma, anxiety, OCD, addiction, ADHD, and the smaller but familiar restlessness of everyday suffering.

chapters.title: Training the attention
chapters.description: Matthew turns to the practice itself: placing attention on the Four Noble Truths, returning from discursive thought, and gathering the mind into something steadier and less shaken.

chapters.title: Poise and clarity
chapters.description: He describes the settled mind as less sticky, less mesmerized, and more at ease, and notes that samadhi and mindfulness are not rigidly separate in the Buddha's teaching.

chapters.title: Seeing suffering clearly
chapters.description: With stabilized attention, suffering becomes more vivid and the facts of clinging, hatred, and greed are harder to avoid or romanticize.

chapters.title: Insights that stick
chapters.description: He closes by saying that attention training gives the mind depth and lets insights leave deeper impressions, so what is understood in practice is less easily overwritten by later life.

title: Guided Meditation: Landing in the Body Breathing
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation emphasizes landing attention in the body and breathing, then using love and awareness to meet whatever arises without turning experience into an enemy. Matthew Brensilver gently widens the practice from the body to the emotional patterns behind worry and the urge to fix and secure life, then returns again and again to the breath. The talk closes by framing mindfulness as a deep surrender that privileges awareness over projects.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling into body breathing, meeting worry with love, and surrendering the drive to fix and secure experience.

chapters.title: Opening and settling
chapters.description: Matthew greets the group, names the format, and invites everyone to sit and begin the practice together.

chapters.title: Love as reference
chapters.description: He introduces love as a helpful reference point: not a feeling to manufacture, but a way of trusting that experience can soften rather than harden the heart.

chapters.title: Breath through the body
chapters.description: The meditation shifts into spreading the breath through the whole body and gathering attention into the felt sense of breathing.

chapters.title: Gentle discipline
chapters.description: He encourages a soft but purposeful attention that does not make experience into an enemy, while keeping the body-breath as the main anchor.

chapters.title: Meeting the urge to fix
chapters.description: The practice turns toward the impulse to secure life, noticing how worry and planning live in the emotional body and the circuits of thought.

chapters.title: Returning to breathing
chapters.description: After bathing the reactive energy in awareness and patience, he suggests coming back to the body breathing when it feels appropriate.

chapters.title: Mindfulness as surrender
chapters.description: In closing reflections, he describes mindful practice as privileging awareness over projects and as a deep kind of surrender.

title: Dharmette: The Mechanisms of Action of the Dharma
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames Dharma practice as having a “mechanism of action,” borrowing the language of clinical research to ask not only whether the path works, but how it works. He moves through classical Buddhist framings—ethical conduct, collectedness, and wisdom—then adds a promised alternative map for the rest of the week. The talk stays practical and encouraging, with brief references to Rilke, Twain, and Bob Dylan used to underline perseverance, transparency, and refuge.
short_summary: A short Dharma talk on how practice changes us, why that matters, and what keeps us going.

chapters.title: Why Ask How It Works
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the clinical question of efficacy versus mechanism and connects it to the Dharma’s own claim that practice works.

chapters.title: Practice as Change
