The talk also draws on other teachers and texts to show how practice can exceed what we thought we were signing up for.
short_summary: A retreat talk that reads Jumping Mouse as a map of practice, from first doubt to surrender and refuge.

chapters.title: Opening the retreat
chapters.description: Matthew sets the tone for the retreat and explains why he chose to begin with a bedtime story.

chapters.title: Why stories matter
chapters.description: He introduces Jumping Mouse, reflects on story and metaphor in Buddhism, and invites listeners to see themselves in the characters.

chapters.title: Hearing the river
chapters.description: The first part of the story becomes a teaching on doubt, curiosity, courage, and the first movement toward practice.

chapters.title: Walking with raccoon
chapters.description: Matthew interprets the journey to the river as a path that disorients us and cannot simply be used to improve the old self.

chapters.title: River and mountain
chapters.description: He expands the story through the metta chant, the frog’s instruction, the sacred mountains, and the warning not to get stuck in a merely comfortable place.

chapters.title: Choosing the wider path
chapters.description: The later journey turns toward deeper renunciation, including the buffalo and the loss of an eye as part of a widening of vision.

chapters.title: Views and surrender
chapters.description: Matthew shifts into teachings on perception, letting go of views, renunciation, and the way the Dharma asks for more than adding practice onto ordinary life.

chapters.title: Refuge and closing
chapters.description: He closes with Mahasi Sayadaw and Bhikkhu Bodhi on openness, refuge, and how small moments of clarity carry larger consequences.

title: Equanimity and Samadhi
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (Vallecitos Mountain Retreat Center) Substantial overlap with previous talk on samadhi.
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores how equanimity and samadhi grow from letting go rather than from effortful control. He moves from the Buddha’s language of clinging and non-clinging into a practical discussion of worry, self-referential thought, and the ways modern attention gets fragmented. The talk gradually turns toward steadiness, embodied concentration, and a softer relationship to experience, ending with a quiet emphasis on surrender, simplicity, and peace.
short_summary: A grounded talk on how samadhi and equanimity arise from non-clinging, patience, and a willingness to let experience be what it is.

chapters.title: Resting In Silence
chapters.description: The talk opens by asking how we come to rest and find refuge, framing silence as something that beckons surrender.

chapters.title: Clinging And Safety
chapters.description: Matthew turns to the Buddha’s teaching on clinging, showing how the search for security often moves through grasping at conditions.

chapters.title: When Consciousness Lands
chapters.description: Using the Ati Raga Sutta’s image of light not landing anywhere, he describes non-clinging as a kind of peace.

chapters.title: Samadhi As Fruit
chapters.description: Samadhi is presented as the fruit of letting go, not something forced into existence by will or by judging the mind's activity.

chapters.title: Restlessness And Worry
chapters.description: He connects samadhi with the body’s settling and with the deeper undercurrent of worry, impermanence, and being constantly stimulated.

chapters.title: The Self At Rest
chapters.description: A citation from Jennifer Beer helps him describe the resting mind’s self-evaluating habit and how concentration loosens that pattern.

chapters.title: Thought Without Shame
chapters.description: Matthew emphasizes that thoughts are not enemies, while also questioning the faith we place in thinking to solve everything.

chapters.title: Equanimity And Pleasure
chapters.description: He broadens the discussion to equanimity and quotes the Buddha on samadhi as a lake fed from below, pleasurable but not to be fetishized.

chapters.title: Practice In Daily Life
chapters.description: The closing section warns against chasing past experiences, then offers practical guidance on patience, restraint, noting, and surrendering to stillness.

title: Guided Meditation: How the world impacts the emotional body
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation that begins with a brief reflection on how the body carries emotional response, then moves into a long period of practice centered on breath, hands, and the felt sense of emotion. He emphasizes that sound, memory, pain, irritation, grief, and restlessness all register in the emotional body, and that meeting those sensations with steady awareness can soften the heart and make mindfulness a refuge. The talk closes by widening the teaching into daily life, including urgency, self-judgment, and the idea that practice unfolds in many chapters rather than one fixed method.
short_summary: A guided meditation on meeting emotional sensations in the body with equanimity, patience, and steady mindfulness.

chapters.title: Opening reflection
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the talk as both reflection and guided practice, then begins distinguishing ordinary bodily sensation from emotional responsivity.

chapters.title: Clinging and the body
chapters.description: He frames clinging as central to suffering and explains why emotional sensations in the body are so important to notice.

chapters.title: Making peace with feeling
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward welcoming grief, memory, pleasure, and aversion without collapsing into thought or resistance.

chapters.title: Mindfulness as refuge
chapters.description: He names mindfulness as a refuge and prepares the transition from reflection into formal guided practice.

chapters.title: Breath and hands
chapters.description: The practice begins with breath awareness and a simple rhythm of inhale, exhale, hands to keep attention moving and grounded.

chapters.title: Emotional circuits
chapters.description: Attention turns to sensations that respond to sound, memory, and thinking, with repeated invitations to notice what is arising.

chapters.title: Permission and contact
chapters.description: He emphasizes allowing sensations to arise, intensify, fade, and vanish, and notes that deeper contact with the emotional body can lessen loneliness.

chapters.title: Extended stillness
chapters.description: A long stretch of quiet practice follows, with repeated prompts to stay aware of bodily feeling, restlessness, and the urge to fix things.

chapters.title: Grief and daily life
chapters.description: The talk closes by connecting patience, grief, and letting go, then offering the emotional body as a reference point for the rest of the day.

title: Guided Meditation: Body Settling, Pleasure of the Breath
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins by meeting the fear and uncertainty that can arise at the start of retreat, and gently reframes practice as a movement toward love and safety. Matthew then leads a body-based settling, balancing alertness with relaxation, and gradually narrows attention to the breath. The talk emphasizes subtle pleasure in breathing, the ways thought pulls attention away, and the invitation to let go without forcing anything.
short_summary: A guided body-and-breath meditation that moves from easing retreat fear into settled awareness, breath sensitivity, and the quiet recognition of pleasure and release.

chapters.title: Meeting Fear with Love
chapters.description: The meditation opens by naming retreat anxiety and offering love as the felt response to fear.

chapters.title: Posture and Grounding
chapters.description: Attention turns to posture, balancing a strong uprightness with softness and surrender to gravity.

chapters.title: Warming the Body
chapters.description: The body is gradually filled with awareness, including places that feel numb or less accessible.

chapters.title: Finding the Breath
chapters.description: Breath sensations are brought into the wider field of body awareness and welcomed without strain.

chapters.title: Sustaining Contact
chapters.description: The guidance steadies attention on the full cycle of inhalation and exhalation, with repeated reminders to let go.

chapters.title: Pleasure in Breathing
chapters.description: The talk shifts to subtle pleasure in breathing as a way to soothe body and mind.

chapters.title: Thought and Letting Go
chapters.description: Matthew names the pull of thinking and the difficulty of letting the rest of experience sit in the background.

chapters.title: Simple Return
chapters.description: The instruction becomes very simple: notice beginning and end, breathe in, breathe out, and relax right now.

chapters.title: After the Bell
chapters.description: The closing invites curiosity about the mind after meditation ends and notices how much unnecessary bracing can soften.

title: The Role of Sila, Samadhi and Panna in Interpersonal Life
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores how sila, samadhi, and panna show up in everyday relationship, not just on retreat. He begins with learning as a lifelong, whole-life path, then turns to vulnerability, speech, feedback, and the ways ego hardens around contact with others. The talk closes by showing how loosening self-view can make us more trustworthy, more open, and more able to love without fixing or clinging.
short_summary: A talk on how ethics, concentration, and wisdom become visible in speech, feedback, and the way we meet other people.

chapters.title: Whole-life learning
chapters.description: Krishnamurti’s quote frames Dharma as an ongoing process of learning that includes the full range of life.

chapters.title: Insight that generalizes
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on how understanding often remains partial and how practice asks us to carry insight into ordinary behavior.

chapters.title: Vulnerability and dependence
chapters.description: Using Martha Nussbaum, he describes human incompleteness, emotion, and the pressure that leads to clinging and self-protection.

chapters.title: Relationship amplifies practice
chapters.description: He looks at groups, friendship, competition, and speech as places where both wholesome qualities and defilements become stronger.

chapters.title: Trust, speech, and feedback
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward becoming a refuge for others, speaking carefully, and staying open to correction without collapsing into defensiveness.

chapters.title: Samadhi as agility
chapters.description: Rebecca Solnit’s emphasis on equality and listening leads into a discussion of steadiness, subtle mind states, and staying with experience instead of story.

chapters.title: Less self-curation
chapters.description: Matthew explores how relaxing self-view makes relationships less tense and more nourishing, including the role of playful honesty and being seen.

chapters.title: Not creating others
chapters.description: Chuang Tzu’s empty boat and Ajahn Sumedho’s advice to 'not create them' deepen the point that love stays open when people are not frozen into fixed identities.

chapters.title: Closing blessing
chapters.description: He ends with a brief sit and a blessing that these practices lead to wisdom, love, and deeper enjoyment of others.

title: Guided Meditation: The Ground of Loving Acceptance
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a guided meditation on how the sense of self keeps seeking ground through activity, and how that search can soften when met with loving acceptance. The practice moves from noticing strain and self-fixing, to resting in breath and body, to working directly with feeling tone and the emotional body as the place where clinging gathers. Near the end, he turns toward ease, non-striving, and a quieter relationship with whatever is present, closing with a Mary Oliver line about letting the body love what it loves.
short_summary: A guided meditation on resting the self’s striving in loving acceptance, feeling tone, and embodied ease.

chapters.title: Selfing and strain
chapters.description: The talk opens by naming the restlessness of self-construction and the stress of always trying to work things out.

chapters.title: Loving acceptance begins
chapters.description: He introduces intentionality and a simple sense of open, grounded, caring attention as the first taste of acceptance.

chapters.title: Finding the ground
chapters.description: The meditation rests in the recognition that the ground is not in strategies or identity, but in receiving oneself and one’s strategies with kindness.

chapters.title: No pressure to be someone
chapters.description: In silence and reflection, he points to the relief of not needing to prove, curate, or become anyone.

chapters.title: Feeling tone and clinging
chapters.description: He explains that emotional sensations and vedana are where clinging starts, and that waking to them can short-circuit suffering.

chapters.title: Breathing into feeling
chapters.description: The practice turns toward breath, body, sound, and metta, then directly touches the urge to fix, own, improve, or protect.

chapters.title: Softening the body
chapters.description: He broadens the view to the body’s front axis and the emotional circuits that carry longing, then uses a Mary Oliver quotation to frame non-striving.

chapters.title: Ease and closing
chapters.description: The final section emphasizes relaxation, minimal effort, and a simple instruction to stop anything that feels unsustainable before ending with a quiet reminder to stay close to oneself.

title: Removing the Thorns of Memory and Becoming
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores memory as something practice does not erase but gently transforms. He moves from present-moment awareness into the way memory, habit, and autobiography shape suffering, then widens to mortality and the craving to become something else. The talk settles into a quiet argument that safety, relinquishment, and openness can let the past be digested and the future feel less fearsome.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on memory, self, and the wish to become, moving toward relinquishment and a less fearful relation to the future.

chapters.title: Opening and context
chapters.description: He greets the group, speaks about tailoring instruction to different practitioners, and frames the talk as something listeners will need to take in for themselves.

chapters.title: Presence and memory
chapters.description: He questions what it means to be present, then shifts from stepping outside time to a practice of letting memory and planning arise within awareness.

chapters.title: Memory in the brain
chapters.description: Using Elie Wiesel and a neuroscience study, he argues that memory is central to both personal healing and the way thought begins before it becomes explicit.

chapters.title: Rewriting the past
chapters.description: He brings in Yalom and Philip Roth to show how the past is constructed, how autobiography can harden, and how practice loosens the grip of self-story.

chapters.title: What we become
chapters.description: With Thich Nhat Hanh, Freud, and Ajahn Sucitto, he shows how the present carries the past and how what is not fully heard can become identity and repetition.

chapters.title: Undigested harm
chapters.description: He tells a childhood story of sibling rivalry to illustrate jealousy, cruelty, and the way practice brings buried harm back into view so it can be felt and unwound.

chapters.title: Blessing the past
chapters.description: He describes practice as blessing former selves and relates this to Kore-eda’s film After Life, where one precious memory is chosen before disappearing into it.

chapters.title: Fear and becoming
chapters.description: He turns to mortality, existence bias, and the Buddha’s teaching on bhava tanha to question the deep drive to secure a future and become someone.

chapters.title: Freedom as release
chapters.description: Citing Ajahn Sumedho, Sariputra, and Ajahn Sucitto, he presents nibbana as the cessation of becoming and practice as a gradual settling into safety, relinquishment, and a freer relation to life.

title: Guided Meditation: Settling through Love, Deepening through Sound
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver guides a quiet meditation that begins with reflection on love as the force that holds life together and gradually turns toward felt experience, sound, and awareness. The practice moves from softening the heart to opening the field of listening, including the inner sound and the way thoughts can be heard as sound. In the final section, he returns to refuge, self-story, and a sustainable way of practicing without heroics.
short_summary: A guided meditation that softens through love and deepens into open listening, then returns to refuge and sustainable practice.

chapters.title: Arriving Through Love
chapters.description: The meditation begins with reflection on how much life depends on love and uses that to help the mind settle.

chapters.title: Softening the Heart
chapters.description: He invites a gentle softening at the heart center and emphasizes that love can relax resistance, worry, and holding.

chapters.title: Kindness Without Pretense
chapters.description: The practice is framed as seasoning the mind with care rather than forcing a feeling, while allowing whatever needs to be felt.

chapters.title: Opening to Sound
chapters.description: Attention widens to the soundscape, with guidance on noticing sound, silence, and the possibility of an inner tonal anchor.

chapters.title: Sound as One Field
chapters.description: He explores hearing thoughts as another kind of sound and resting in a single field where inside and outside begin to collapse.

chapters.title: Rumi on Wholeness
chapters.description: A brief poetic section uses Rumi to encourage wholehearted commitment rather than half-measures or delay.

chapters.title: Refuge and Self-Story
chapters.description: He returns to the question of refuge, contrasting the Three Jewels with the habitual refuge in the story of me.

chapters.title: Resting Sustainably
chapters.description: The talk closes with a gentle reminder to be slow, sustain the effort, and let practice continue through ordinary transitions.

title: Seven Factors of Awakening
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver lays out the seven factors of awakening as a living sequence rather than a checklist, beginning with mindfulness and investigation and moving through energy, delight, tranquility, concentration, and equanimity. He keeps returning to the feel of practice in the body and mind: how curiosity opens inquiry, how effort becomes relaxed perseverance, and how equanimity makes room for the messiness of human life. Several brief references — including Sīdō Tejaniya, Gil Fronsdal, Sharon Salzberg, Leonard Cohen, Heather Havrilesky, Michelle McDonald, and Shinzen Young — help ground the teaching in concrete examples and personal stories.
short_summary: Matthew Brensilver explores the seven factors of awakening as a practical, unfolding relationship between mindfulness, inquiry, energy, joy, calm, collectedness, and equanimity.

chapters.title: Opening into release
chapters.description: He begins with a small personal example to frame the path as one of release rather than acquisition and to set up the talk’s focus on suffering and opening.

chapters.title: Seven factors overview
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the seven factors, explains the movement from hindrances to awakening, and cites the Buddha story of asking for the factors during illness.

chapters.title: Learning mindfulness
chapters.description: He describes mindfulness as something learned through practice, not a fixed technique, and develops it as awareness of awareness.

chapters.title: Equanimity and balance
chapters.description: The talk turns to equanimity as the balance that keeps mindfulness from becoming brittle or reactive, including the idea of the mind’s relative balance among factors.

chapters.title: Softening into experience
chapters.description: He explores how equanimity and mindfulness reduce craving and aversion in the act of noticing, and uses a Leonard Cohen line as a brief image of radical openness.

chapters.title: Tolerating ambivalence
chapters.description: A long Heather Havrilesky quotation illustrates the ordinary mess of love, irritation, and judgment, which Matthew uses to show equanimity as tolerance for human ambivalence.

chapters.title: Curiosity and investigation
chapters.description: Matthew develops dhammavicaya as curiosity without agenda, cites the Buddha’s four responses to pain, and tells a retreat story with Michelle McDonald about hidden defensiveness.

chapters.title: Energy, delight, calm
chapters.description: He distinguishes investigation from rumination, then moves through energy as persistence, delight as a sense of homecoming, and tranquility as the heart’s relief from stimulation.

chapters.title: Collected mind, not thought
chapters.description: The final movement shows how samadhi steadies listening, shifts practice from philosophy into lived path, and ends by contrasting compulsive thinking with knowing life as experience.

title: Guided Meditation: Settling into the Pleasure of the Breath
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver leads a guided meditation that begins with settling the body and sensing the breath as a source of quiet pleasure. The practice then widens into a way of using that pleasure to signal safety, soften the heart, and stay with experience without sorting it into good and bad. In the latter part of the talk he turns to walking meditation, describing it as a bridge into daily life and a place to work gently with boredom, continuity, and attention.
short_summary: A guided meditation on finding ease in the breath, then extending that steadiness into walking practice and everyday awareness.

chapters.title: Settling the body
chapters.description: The meditation opens by gathering awareness into the whole body and finding a posture that balances ease with alertness.

chapters.title: Pleasure in the breath
chapters.description: He invites attention to the subtle satisfactions in inhaling and exhaling, letting the whole body receive and be soothed by them.

chapters.title: Letting awareness flood
chapters.description: The practice emphasizes arriving, resting in the breath cycle, and allowing the body to feel held and supported.

chapters.title: Safety and softening
chapters.description: He speaks of signaling to the deep mind that it is safe to relax and letting experience soften the heart rather than being managed.

chapters.title: Whole-body presence
chapters.description: The instructions return to sensing the whole body breathing and opening wholeheartedly to what is here.

chapters.title: Knowing the breath
chapters.description: A brief instruction anchors attention in direct, simple awareness of breathing in and breathing out.

chapters.title: Walking as continuity
chapters.description: The talk shifts to how sitting and walking support each other and how practice can be carried through the day.

chapters.title: Working with boredom
chapters.description: He explores boredom, narrowed attention, and small mental notes as ways to stay intimate with sensation while walking.

chapters.title: Practical retreat notes
chapters.description: The closing minutes cover alternatives for injury, retreat logistics, and a few gentle reminders about not drawing early conclusions.

title: Reflecting On An Ajahn Sucitto Quote
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on an Ajahn Sucitto teaching about why stopping feels difficult: the self stays busy trying to find ground, while practice asks for a deeper loving acceptance of experience as it is. He moves from busyness and silence to self-comparison, mindfulness, and the way a softer relationship to self can make room for more freedom and less fear. The talk closes with philosophical references and a quiet devotional ending that turns the discussion toward welfare, holding, and non-clinging.
short_summary: A reflective talk on busyness, selfing, and how loving acceptance can become a real ground for practice.

chapters.title: Ajahn Sucitto's quote
chapters.description: Matthew introduces the teaching and begins unpacking the claim that stopping is hard because self is always busy searching for ground.

chapters.title: Busyness as human condition
chapters.description: He broadens the theme into ordinary life, where even quiet time and retreat can feel organized around avoiding stillness.

chapters.title: Solitude and avoidance
chapters.description: Pascal and David Foster Wallace help frame the fear of being alone with the deeper pain that silence can expose.

chapters.title: Self, agitation, and comparison
chapters.description: Matthew turns to the subtle connection between selfing, defensiveness, and the habit of evaluating oneself as an object.

chapters.title: Mind and narrative self
chapters.description: He uses Nisargadatta and Dennett to suggest that what feels like a solid self is more like a narrative center folded out of experience.

chapters.title: Loving the mess
chapters.description: A long example from Heather Havrilesky and references to teachers like Ajahn Chah and Ajahn Sumedho support a gentler, less combative way of meeting our own messiness.

chapters.title: Non-dwelling and mortality
chapters.description: The talk shifts toward not dwelling in self at all, then toward death, Parfit, and the relief that comes when the self is less of a prison.

chapters.title: Closing blessing
chapters.description: Matthew ends with repeated invocation, a blessing for welfare, and a brief closing exchange with the group.

title: Impressionistic vs Precise Technique
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores the tension between impressionistic and precise meditation instructions, arguing that practice needs both openness and discernment. He moves from a broad reflection on how teaching works into a clear three-part method for noticing feeling, images, and inner talk, then shows how that triad can help untangle selfing and support healing. The talk returns repeatedly to memory, pain, and the possibility of blessing the past through patient, attentive practice.
short_summary: A talk on balancing flexible and precise meditation technique, centered on feeling, image, and inner talk as a way to understand self and heal memory.

chapters.title: Teaching and technique
chapters.description: Matthew names his ambivalence about teaching meditation and lays out the pull between vague, impressionistic guidance and precise, prescribed instruction.

chapters.title: Learning and loving
chapters.description: He offers the talk’s main orientation: practice is for learning and loving, and for not squandering suffering.

chapters.title: Memory shapes the present
chapters.description: Using Elie Wiesel, he argues that the present is formed by the past and that mindful attention can bless what is unfinished.

chapters.title: Three channels of experience
chapters.description: He introduces a more precise framework, drawn from Shinzen Young, distinguishing feeling in the body, internal images, and mental chatter.

chapters.title: Thought and selfing
chapters.description: Matthew shows how thought can dominate experience, create a worldview, and obscure impermanence and not-self.

chapters.title: Noting with clarity
chapters.description: He describes how to use casual or formal noting to track the three channels, including subtle emotions like impatience.

chapters.title: Blessing the past
chapters.description: The practice is framed as a quiet digestion of memory: meeting what arises now without compulsively rewriting the autobiography.

chapters.title: Questions and release
chapters.description: In the closing exchange he revisits redemption, allows room for sorrow with Leonard Cohen’s line, and ends with a gentler, less controlled sense of practice.

title: Monday Night Dharma Talk
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames mindfulness as a living curiosity rather than a duty, arguing that practice begins where the student actually is. He then develops that theme through investigation, honesty, and the willingness to look closely at craving, delusion, secrecy, shame, and the ways we manage our self-image. The talk closes by turning outward: other people, especially trusted feedback, help reveal blind spots and make the heart more transparent and trustworthy.
short_summary: A talk on curiosity as the engine of practice, and on how honest investigation loosens self-deception and opens the heart.

chapters.title: Curiosity Over Discipline
chapters.description: Brensilver begins by contrasting curiosity with discipline and saying practice needs a personal spark rather than mere adherence.

chapters.title: The Engine of Practice
chapters.description: He uses Tejaniya’s framing of the seven factors of awakening to show that mindfulness, investigation, and energy are the causes, while the calmer factors are effects.

chapters.title: Do Not Force Interest
chapters.description: He describes how practice cannot be faked or imposed, and brings in Michelle McDonald to suggest that painful material should only be approached when genuine interest is present.

chapters.title: Investigation as Learning
chapters.description: The talk broadens into the difference between passive listening and active inquiry, including Joseph Goldstein’s line about not knowing one has a mind before practice.

chapters.title: Seeing Suffering Clearly
chapters.description: He turns to greed, aversion, and delusion, then to the three characteristics, emphasizing that investigation means seeing experience as fleeting, ungovernable, and not worth clinging to.

chapters.title: Inquiry and Distance
chapters.description: Brensilver distinguishes investigation from rumination and draws on a psychology idea of self-distancing to describe how meditation can create enough space to look without becoming detached.

chapters.title: Secrets and Honesty
chapters.description: He explores secrecy, shame, and deception, using Bella DePaulo, Sam Harris, Rilke, and Gil Fronsdal to argue that mindfulness is a practice of honesty and transparency.

chapters.title: Feedback and Disorientation
chapters.description: The final section emphasizes learning from other people, especially criticism, and becoming willing to feel disoriented as the old story of self loosens.

title: Practice Note: Note your life away
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a brief practice note on noting: silently labeling experience to steady attention, but also to shift how experience is held in awareness. He develops the idea that what is noted is no longer quite “our life,” so practice becomes a way of stepping out of identification and seeing experience as arising and passing. The talk closes by widening the lens to body, room, feeling, and thought, ending with the phrase that we may be “noting our life away.”
short_summary: A short reflection on noting practice as both a tool for attention and a way of loosening identification with experience.

chapters.title: Opening frame
chapters.description: The talk is introduced as a practice note from the Insight Meditation Center.

chapters.title: Noting as awareness
chapters.description: Brensilver explains noting as silently acknowledging experience and letting awareness soak into it.

chapters.title: More than stabilization
chapters.description: He says noting is not only a way to steady attention but also has a deeper function in the mind.

chapters.title: Stepping off identification
chapters.description: What is noted is described as no longer being exactly our life, so attention begins to loosen its grip.

chapters.title: Everything can be noted
chapters.description: He names body, room, gravity, emotion, heart, and thinking as all available to awareness.

chapters.title: Noting life away
chapters.description: The practice is summed up as taking no part for granted and seeing life as arising and vanishing experience.

chapters.title: Closing wish
chapters.description: The talk ends with a brief, lightly self-aware closing wish for the morning.

title: Dharmette: Maturing our autobiography
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores how mindfulness helps us step out of self-reinforcing stories without dismissing the value of narrative altogether. He argues that practice can mature our autobiography: making our life story more coherent, less rigid, and gentler toward what was once too painful or confusing to hold. The talk moves from neuroimaging and the limits of rumination into a quieter account of remembering, reinterpreting, and blessing the past with Dharma.
short_summary: A talk on how practice can loosen identification with story while also helping us rewrite our life in a more spacious, humane way.

chapters.title: Stepping out of narrative
chapters.description: He opens with mindfulness as a way of disengaging from ruminative, self-relevant story and returning to direct experience.

chapters.title: Why stories limit
chapters.description: Using a quotation about storytelling animals, he shows how identity can become claustrophobic when we mistake thought for reality.

chapters.title: Space around thought
chapters.description: He contrasts identification with thoughts and feelings with the relief of knowing them as experiences that can be held at a distance.

chapters.title: Maturing the story
chapters.description: The talk turns to the redemptive side of story, suggesting that practice can make our self-narrative more rich, coherent, and nuanced.

chapters.title: A jagged autobiography
chapters.description: He describes how practice brings up unfinished or undigested experiences, and how simplistic judgments can shape the way we tell our lives.

chapters.title: Memory in practice
chapters.description: Bringing past experience into stillness, he says meditation lets old pain and grief arise now, where they can begin to lose some of their charge.

chapters.title: Remembering with Dharma
chapters.description: He links memory, forgiveness, and reinterpretation, suggesting that practice helps us bless the past with wisdom and love.

chapters.title: Putting the story down
chapters.description: The close emphasizes integration: the past feels more like the past, life feels less ambush-prone, and the autobiography can finally be set down more safely.

title: guided meditation
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (New York Insight Meditation Center) Meditation practice cultivates a diverse set of attentional, emotional and introspective skills. Central to the practice of mindfulness is the stabilization of attention. But before our attention stabilizes, practice can be overstimulating. This talk will explore the process through which the mind comes to rest. In developing this steadiness, equanimity (the capacity to fully permit the flow of both pleasure and pain) is a vital skill. We will see how concentration and equanimity reinforce each other and support a deeper understanding of ourselves. And how this stability, in turn, makes space for the heart to respond with joy and compassion.
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a guided meditation centered on settling the nervous system, stabilizing attention, and meeting the usual friction of practice with patience. He moves from posture and breathing instructions into ways of working with fatigue, agitation, and the tendency to make a meditation into a future project. The talk gradually widens into a gentle inquiry into self, fear, and the quiet that can come when attention is steady enough to rest.
short_summary: A guided meditation on settling, steadiness, and making peace with the mind’s wandering.

chapters.title: Settling the posture
chapters.description: He begins by inviting a sustainable posture that supports both relaxation and alertness.

chapters.title: Entering practice gently
chapters.description: He contrasts a direct drop into technique with a softer unfurling into meditation when the mind feels jarring or busy.

chapters.title: Root and support
chapters.description: Attention turns to the spine, gravity, and the body’s sense of being both uplifted and held.

chapters.title: Breath and energy
chapters.description: He suggests emphasizing the inhale or exhale depending on fatigue or agitation, letting the breath steady the system.

chapters.title: Making peace with imperfection
chapters.description: The instruction widens into a practice of not sorting everything out, but allowing the human condition to be incomplete.

chapters.title: This life, not rehearsal
chapters.description: He points to the tendency to live as if life will begin later, then brings attention back to lived sensation now.

chapters.title: Choosing the field of attention
chapters.description: He names possible anchors—breath, sound, body, or a broader field—and emphasizes knowing the boundary of attention and forgiving wandering.

chapters.title: Steadying the inner gaze
chapters.description: He describes how thought can be accompanied by an inner visual field and invites a gentle stabilization behind closed eyes.

chapters.title: Scanning for clinging
chapters.description: The closing scan looks for tension and clinging, ending with a reflection on fear, self, and the settling that allows the mind to rest.

title: Compulsive Thinking, Concentration & Equanimity
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
description: (New York Insight Meditation Center) Meditation practice cultivates a diverse set of attentional, emotional and introspective skills. Central to the practice of mindfulness is the stabilization of attention. But before our attention stabilizes, practice can be overstimulating. This talk will explore the process through which the mind comes to rest. In developing this steadiness, equanimity (the capacity to fully permit the flow of both pleasure and pain) is a vital skill. We will see how concentration and equanimity reinforce each other and support a deeper understanding of ourselves. And how this stability, in turn, makes space for the heart to respond with joy and compassion.
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores why the mind so often feels restless, fragmented, and compelled to think its way through everything, especially in a culture that rewards constant stimulation. He argues that concentration and equanimity develop together: steadiness softens the urge to control experience, and equanimity makes room for feeling without friction. The talk moves from the ordinary habits of worry and self-narration into a quieter, more practical account of letting go, where peace is not numbness but a less defensive way of meeting life.
short_summary: A grounded talk on how concentration and equanimity support each other in loosening compulsive thinking and making room for peace.

chapters.title: What experience becomes
chapters.description: Matthew begins by saying that our life is shaped by conscious experience and that gathered attention makes life feel richer.

chapters.title: Samadhi as letting go
chapters.description: He explains concentration as the fruit of release, and contrasts it with the restless habit of always trying to solve life.

chapters.title: Worry and modern life
chapters.description: The talk turns to worry, technology, and evolutionary vigilance, with references to Bhikkhu Bodhi and Borkovec supporting the argument.

chapters.title: A sheltered mind
chapters.description: Matthew describes samadhi as a temporary shelter from the storm and says a safe ethical community helps the mind settle.

chapters.title: Thought’s false gravity
chapters.description: He looks closely at mind wandering, self-evaluation, and the way thoughts seem more important than they are, drawing on Malia Mason, Jennifer Beer, and Daniel Kahneman.

chapters.title: Silence without defense
chapters.description: The discussion deepens into disorientation, autobiography, and equanimity as 'gazing upon without interference,' which keeps life poignant without making it melodramatic.

chapters.title: Pleasure, attachment, and restraint
chapters.description: Matthew warns against compulsively optimizing experience and tells Joseph Goldstein's story as a caution about attachment to blissful concentration states.

chapters.title: Enough, and waking up
chapters.description: He closes by reframing dana, quoting Suzuki Roshi, and using a leaf-and-stillness image to show that peace comes from non-interference rather than force.

chapters.title: Closing reflections
chapters.description: Matthew ends with a brief summary: letting go, equanimity, forgiveness, and making peace with the imperfection of being human.

title: Reflections on Sila
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on sila as more than a list of rules: a lived path of non-harming, self-knowledge, humility, and the willingness to be changed by suffering. He moves from the image of becoming a refuge for all beings into questions of grief, forgiveness, emptiness, and how ethical life deepens as the mind becomes more unified. The talk then turns toward modern responsibility, including the pull between self-protection and widening concern for others, animals, and future generations.
short_summary: A quiet, searching talk on sila as non-harming, ethical growth, and the widening of care.

chapters.title: Sila as refuge
chapters.description: Matthew begins with his written reflection on becoming a refuge for all beings and develops it into a view of ethics, safety, and compassion as the heart of the path.

chapters.title: Safety and self-knowledge
chapters.description: He explains how being safe for others depends on seeing the roots of harm, including greed, hatred, delusion, and the need for humility and sensitivity.

chapters.title: Letting go and grief
chapters.description: The talk turns to the way letting go can feel like grief, including the pain of recognizing harm done by us and to us, and the importance of not squandering suffering.

chapters.title: Universal and personal
chapters.description: Matthew holds together the shared human condition and the idiosyncratic ways each person meets the path, emphasizing that ethical growth is improvisational and autobiographical.

chapters.title: Heart, karma, and love
chapters.description: He describes how mindfulness of goodness and pain both bring love, how sila and samadhi deepen one another, and how a steadier mind can take on the coloration of care.

chapters.title: Emptiness as nonviolence
chapters.description: With a Nagarjuna passage, he links emptiness to radical nonviolence and argues that harm loosens when grasping and the sense of solidity in experience begin to fade.

chapters.title: What goodness requires
chapters.description: The talk widens into social and moral questions about what we owe, using Peter Singer to question self-justifying ethics and to challenge easy limits on responsibility.

chapters.title: Shelter and enough
chapters.description: Matthew acknowledges exhaustion, quotes Dylan on needing shelter from the storm, and returns to the question of when we have enough stability to serve more fully.

chapters.title: Completeness and offering
chapters.description: He closes with a quiet reflection on death, completeness, and the wish that the goodness of practice overflow into the lives of others.

title: Technology as an Existential Threat to Mindfulness - Matthew Brensilver and Randy Fernando
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver and Randy Fernando examine how smartphones, apps, and the attention economy shape mindfulness, restlessness, and even our sense of self. The first half builds the case that modern technology trains habit, urgency, and fragmentation; the second half turns practical, with suggestions for simpler defaults, gentler boundaries, and more humane ways of using devices. A short guided experiment and discussion sections help make the point concrete without turning the talk into a polemic.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on how technology trains attention, habit, and urgency—and what more humane use might look like.

chapters.title: Arriving and settling
chapters.description: The meeting opens with brief welcome remarks, a few quiet minutes of sitting, and the transition into the afternoon.

chapters.title: Attention and mind wandering
chapters.description: Matthew begins by naming the question of technology, then frames mindfulness as a balancing factor while allowing for a middle way with mind wandering.

chapters.title: Time slipping away
chapters.description: He cites Jonathan Schooler and David Whyte to show how distraction and unconsciousness can let years pass almost unnoticed.

chapters.title: Phones as training
chapters.description: The talk turns to phones as pseudo-companions that soothe restlessness while quietly training attention, loneliness, and habits of response.

chapters.title: What tech rewards
chapters.description: Matthew describes technology as a system that rewards fragmentation, urgency, and egoic posting, and notes how this often shows up in ordinary social media use.

chapters.title: Division and deepfakes
chapters.description: The focus widens to political manipulation, the breakdown of shared facts, and the threat of fake media, before returning to the larger claim that some technology now undermines mindfulness itself.

chapters.title: Safer products and band-aids
chapters.description: In questions, he distinguishes humane tools from manipulative ones and treats screen-time apps and maps as partial aids rather than true solutions.

chapters.title: Mindlessness and guilt
chapters.description: He responds to questions about zoning out, shame, and the edge between wholesome rest and compulsive checking, emphasizing less guilt and more clear noticing.

chapters.title: Practical habits
chapters.description: The discussion moves toward resilience, family dynamics, and concrete changes like notification hygiene, grayscale, reduced texting, and not checking the phone first thing.

title: Monday Night Dharma Talk
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores grief as a central mechanism of Dharma practice: not a detour from awakening, but part of how the heart matures through letting go. He moves from a retreat on anger that revealed grief, through poetry and teacher quotations, into a clear argument that non-clinging often feels like loss before it feels like freedom. The talk widens from personal practice to collective life, then closes with comments and a short period of sitting that emphasizes radical permission for whatever the heart is carrying.
short_summary: A talk on how grief, rather than being separate from practice, can be one of the main ways the heart opens and wisdom deepens.

chapters.title: Three Questions
chapters.description: Matthew frames the talk around three questions about mindfulness, usefulness, and mechanism, then says he will focus on how practice actually works.

chapters.title: Grief on Retreat
chapters.description: He tells the story of teaching an anger retreat where the room felt more like grief than anger, and uses that experience to name grieving as part of spiritual unfolding.

chapters.title: Why Grief Matters
chapters.description: He argues that consciously feeling grief is liberating and necessary, connecting it to compassion, insight, and the failure to grieve at both personal and collective levels.

chapters.title: Poetry of Loss
chapters.description: Leonard Cohen and Mary Oliver help him show how grief can break the heart open, enlarge love, and make impermanence feel intimate rather than abstract.

chapters.title: Letting Go
chapters.description: Using Joseph Goldstein and Steve Armstrong, he describes non-clinging as the painful unclenching of a fist and the humbling collapse of fantasies about control and identity.

chapters.title: Ownership and Gift
chapters.description: A passage by Andy Olinsky on ownership leads into a wider reflection on greed, delusion, and the way life becomes more like a gift once possession loosens.

chapters.title: No Steps For Grief
chapters.description: He quotes Leonard Cohen and Stephen Levine to show that grief has no fixed method, can only be met partially, and may hurt without damaging the heart.

chapters.title: The Valley of Lost Things
chapters.description: Reading Catherine Schultz, he presents loss as built into reality itself and says that noticing this makes attention, gratitude, and ethics more urgent.

chapters.title: Questions and Closing Sitting
chapters.description: After a brief exchange with the audience about mourning and presence, he leads a short sit and closes by blessing the heart's capacity to be softened by letting go.

title: Sila as Our Practice in the World (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver speaks about sila as the way practice shows up in ordinary life: in attention to suffering, in how we relate to others, and in the habits that shape our conduct. He starts from vulnerability and dependency, then moves through mindfulness, non-clinging, feedback, and the courage to revise our views when they harden into self-protection. The talk closes by widening ethics beyond private self-improvement into concern for others, future generations, and a life that can be of benefit without needing certainty about outcomes.
short_summary: A quiet talk on sila as the lived expression of practice, centered on non-clinging, feedback, and ethical responsibility toward others.

chapters.title: Vulnerability and dependence
chapters.description: Matthew begins with our basic incompleteness, the emotions that arise from it, and the way shame can cover our dependency on others and on the world.

chapters.title: Belonging and mindfulness
chapters.description: He links peace to interdependence, then shifts from trying to remember mindfulness to the deeper training that raises the baseline of awareness over time.

chapters.title: Ethics as the practice
chapters.description: Using Bob Thurman’s joke about practice and the concert, he argues that ethics is where practice enters the world most directly and where attention to suffering matters most.

chapters.title: Love, clinging, and letting go
chapters.description: He explores how sila exposes clinging, how love differs from control, and why goodness and joy are not manufactured so much as revealed when greed and aversion loosen.

chapters.title: Wisdom and humility
chapters.description: Matthew turns to the arhat-bodhisattva contrast and then to overconfidence, arguing for a middle way that combines courage, intellectual humility, and active open-mindedness.

chapters.title: Feedback and self-view
chapters.description: He describes ethical practice as an open system that learns through feedback, even when feedback stings or is imperfect, because it reveals how the self is constructed.

chapters.title: Freefall and courage
chapters.description: The talk deepens into the discomfort of having the self rattled, the defensiveness that follows, and the invitation to tolerate that freefall as part of ethical growth.

chapters.title: Moral incoherence
chapters.description: Drawing on Ajahn Chah and a personal story about money, Matthew reflects on the rare ideal of sainthood and the recurring moments when ordinary life no longer feels fully justifiable.

chapters.title: Radical hearts
chapters.description: He ends by reimagining hope as meaning rather than outcome, widening the frame to collective responsibility and closing with Leonard Cohen’s wish for a treaty between love and anger.

title: Untitled (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver uses Martha Nussbaum’s account of human dependence to open a wider reflection on shame, vulnerability, and ethics. From there he moves through mindfulness, sila, and the way practice shows up in ordinary conduct, arguing that ethical life is where the Dharma becomes most concrete. The talk settles into questions of confidence, feedback, defensiveness, and moral incoherence, and ends by returning to the possibility of a life of benefit without grand certainty.
short_summary: A Dharma talk on vulnerability, ethics, and how practice matures through non-clinging, feedback, and care for others.

chapters.title: Dependence and Shame
chapters.description: Matthew begins with Martha Nussbaum’s account of human incompleteness and the emotions that arise from dependence.

chapters.title: Belonging and Mindfulness
chapters.description: He links interdependence with peace, then shifts from trying to remember mindfulness to the longer-term training of heart and habit.

chapters.title: Ethics as Practice
chapters.description: Using Bob Thurman’s joke about the 'concert,' he argues that ethical life is the real expression of practice and that mindfulness and sila support each other.

chapters.title: Wisdom and Humility
chapters.description: He brings in Gil Fronsdal, Ajahn Sumedho, and research on overconfidence to question rigid views and to point toward a middle way of calibrated confidence.

chapters.title: Feedback and Self-View
chapters.description: Matthew describes ethics as a field guide to clinging, emphasizing feedback, defensiveness, and the way the self becomes visible when it is challenged.

chapters.title: Freefall and Courage
chapters.description: He explores the discomfort of having our self-image rattled and names ethical growth as a courageous willingness to stay with disorientation.

chapters.title: One Fruit of Practice
chapters.description: Ajahn Chah’s 'one fruit' saying frames sila, samadhi, and panna as inseparable, and the talk turns toward responsibility, moral incoherence, and hope that is not dependent on outcomes.

chapters.title: Radical Hearts
chapters.description: The talk closes with the question of how to live for the benefit of others, ending in a quiet appeal for mutual care and reconciliation.

title: Sitting with Instructions (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames retreat practice as a way of fully digesting experience rather than adding something to a life already elsewhere. He moves from the ordinary senses and the mind’s movement, to love as the deeper measure of awakening, to the quiet freedom that comes when practice becomes inseparable from life. In the second half, he turns toward retreat ending: change, integration, tears, and the way a practice can continue to settle after the formal sitting is over.
short_summary: A retreat talk on living experience fully, with love as the measure of awakening and integration as retreat comes to a close.

chapters.title: Experience as life
chapters.description: He begins by grounding practice in direct experience—sights, sounds, sensations, and the movement of mind—as the whole field of our life.

chapters.title: Digesting the day
chapters.description: He describes practice as experiencing life fully enough that nothing is left over, as if the day is gradually digested along the way.

chapters.title: Love as measure
chapters.description: The teaching shifts toward the idea that practice is not about a special quality but about love, the condition without which there is no real chance.

chapters.title: Awakening into love
chapters.description: He quotes Norman Fischer’s poem on awakening and uses it to show how joy, sorrow, clinging, and release all inform love.

chapters.title: Subtle freedom
chapters.description: Drawing again on Norman Fischer, he describes mature practice as quiet and non-dramatic: less self-defense, less need to be somebody, more ordinary freedom.

chapters.title: Endings and change
chapters.description: As the retreat nears its close, he talks about transition, how change affects the mind, and why experience needs time to fold into awareness.

chapters.title: Dreams and letting go
chapters.description: He answers a question about dream practice and notes the Buddhist sense that life can be known as dreamlike, which supports release.

chapters.title: Tears and integration
chapters.description: In response to a question about post-retreat emotion, he speaks about tears as carrying many threads and about the slow integration that follows deep practice.

title: Day 6: Sitting with Instructions (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a quiet instruction for sitting with honesty rather than performance. He moves from noticing urgency and defining the edge of attention, into a longer reflection on impermanence, where the present moment is not something to freeze but something to trust as it changes. The talk then turns toward mettā practice and closes with a practical reminder about continuity of awareness in ordinary life.
short_summary: A grounded retreat talk on honesty in meditation, impermanence, mettā, and continuity of awareness.

chapters.title: Starting the sit
chapters.description: He begins with a gentle body-and-mind scan and points to places where urgency is present.

chapters.title: Not pretending
chapters.description: The teaching emphasizes that meditation does not require forcing acceptance, and that this moment can be met as imperfect yet good enough.

chapters.title: Drawing the boundary
chapters.description: He explains the practical difference between the area of focus and distraction, and why it helps to know exactly where attention is being placed.

chapters.title: Urge and tension
chapters.description: The talk turns to how the mind leans toward the next breath, the next insight, or the future, and how that subtle reaching can be released.

chapters.title: No banks on this river
chapters.description: Using the image of a river without banks, he describes surrendering into change and the flow of anicca rather than freezing the moment.

chapters.title: Offering experience
chapters.description: He shifts from trying to get something from practice to offering oneself to experience and trusting the logic of Dharma.

chapters.title: Turning to mettā
chapters.description: The talk moves into loving-kindness, with repeated phrases of goodwill and a closing reflection on what changes when the bell ends the sit.

chapters.title: Continuity off the cushion
chapters.description: He ends by comparing continuity of awareness to not skipping chapters in a book and then gives retreat announcements.

title: Sīla, Samādhi, Pañña
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames the Buddhist path through sīla, samādhi, and paññā as three inseparable expressions of one training, grounded in love rather than moral performance. He moves from the ordinary hindrances and the Four Noble Truths into a patient account of ethics, collectedness, and wisdom, using images of refuge, the face, and ownership to show how practice softens fear and self-clinging. The talk steadily widens from personal suffering to the way a heart becomes safer, clearer, and more available to others.
short_summary: A grounded teaching on how ethical conduct, mental steadiness, and wisdom support one another as one path of love and release.

chapters.title: Hindrances and response
chapters.description: He opens with the common retreat hindrances and gives a concise set of responses to each, setting a practical tone.

chapters.title: Four Noble Truths
chapters.description: He recounts a conference with the Dalai Lama and Ajahn Amaro's medical framing of diagnosis, cause, prognosis, and treatment for suffering.

chapters.title: One fruit, three trainings
chapters.description: He names the Eightfold Path in its three baskets and uses Ajahn Chah's mango image to show that sīla, samādhi, and paññā are one fruit.

chapters.title: Ethics as refuge
chapters.description: He develops sīla as a wish to be safe for others, drawing on the Buddha's language of refuge and the softening that comes from self-care and direct encounter with the face of another.

chapters.title: Self-judgment loosens
chapters.description: He turns to the suffering of self-hatred, reading Heather Havrilesky as a pointed invitation to leave the imagined better self behind and rest in what is already here.

chapters.title: Collecting the mind
chapters.description: He explains samādhi as gathered stability rather than mere concentration, and shows how settling the mind gives temporary refuge from the relentlessness of change.

chapters.title: Thought and narrative
chapters.description: He questions the urgency of thought, noting how the mind builds narratives for security and how precepts create enough safety to begin letting go.

chapters.title: Wisdom and non-ownership
chapters.description: He arrives at paññā through the surprise of practice, the loosening of delusion, and a critique of ownership as a root of suffering.

chapters.title: Gift, identity, release
chapters.description: He closes by describing life as generosity rather than possession, letting go of identity as refuge, and returning to the simple work of living, serving, and dying.

title: Practice Notes
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver offers a short set of practice notes focused on how to work with the mind in meditation. He emphasizes that practice is often personal and idiosyncratic, and that what helps in one moment may be a reminder, a reassurance, an inquiry, or a gentle check on clinging. The talk closes by pointing back to what is already good and present, rather than turning practice into an endless project of self-improvement.
short_summary: A concise talk on using tailored inner reminders, inquiry, and gentleness to meet practice as it actually is.

chapters.title: Opening Practice Notes
chapters.description: He begins informally and introduces the morning's walking and sitting practice, along with the invitation for one-on-one practice discussions.

chapters.title: Personal Practice Discussions
chapters.description: Brensilver describes practice conversations as a place for whatever is closest to the heart, from technical questions to how the teachings fit daily life.

chapters.title: Why Guidance Must Fit
chapters.description: He reflects on how Dharma is taught broadly but received in highly individual ways, and why practice guidance needs to be tailored.

chapters.title: Learning Inner Pep Talks
chapters.description: The talk turns to the way practitioners gradually learn to give themselves timely, useful reminders that fit their own minds and the present moment.

chapters.title: Energy, Gentleness, Inquiry
chapters.description: He names several kinds of support the mind may need: effort, surrender, compassion, reassurance, or a question that exposes clinging.

chapters.title: Returning To Present Goodness
chapters.description: He warns against turning Dharma into a self-improvement project and suggests asking what goodness is already here so the present can be felt more clearly.

title: Dharmette: The Human Condition
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver reflects on how hard it can be to hold the full range of being human: grief, joy, climate anxiety, small pleasures, and the sense that they don’t neatly fit together. He then describes a meditation-related experience in which tears, laughter, and a surprising underlying peace arose at once, suggesting a more spacious way of meeting suffering. The talk ends with a quiet recognition that the human condition is larger, stranger, and more workable than it first appears.
short_summary: A brief reflection on the difficulty and possibility of holding grief, joy, and spaciousness together in the human condition.

chapters.title: Being Human
chapters.description: He opens with the basic confusion of being human and the challenge of holding joy, sorrow, suffering, and progress together.

chapters.title: One Moment at a Time
chapters.description: He turns to Buddhist psychology and the idea that experience arrives one mind moment at a time, even when life feels internally divided.

chapters.title: What Happens in the News
chapters.description: He describes how news and climate concerns can dominate consciousness and make everything else seem secondary.

chapters.title: A Practice Setting
chapters.description: He introduces an unfamiliar breathing practice in a dark room, setting up the experience that follows.

chapters.title: Tears and Compassion
chapters.description: Hearing a young woman crying nearby, he feels tears of compassion that also touch unfinished places in his own heart.

chapters.title: Laughter in Grief
chapters.description: Alongside sadness, he notices unexpected laughter and a sense of the cosmic joke, a wider perspective on pain.

chapters.title: Peace Underneath
chapters.description: He describes a third simultaneous layer: a deep, steady peace that seemed to hold the tears and laughter together.

chapters.title: The Human Condition
chapters.description: He draws the experience together as a full acknowledgment of the human condition and a quiet acceptance of how much it asks of us.

title: Ethical Implications of Mindfulness
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver speaks about mindfulness as cultivation rather than quick self-assessment, returning again and again to the idea that practice changes us gradually and often invisibly. From there he broadens the talk into ethics: how insight shows up in speech, relationships, generosity, and our sense of responsibility to other beings. The talk moves between personal practice, Buddhist references, and secular moral arguments, ending with a quiet reminder to practice without needing applause.
short_summary: A talk on mindfulness as gradual cultivation that unfolds into ethics, relationship, and responsibility.

chapters.title: Measuring Progress
chapters.description: Matthew begins by challenging the habit of checking after every breath to see whether meditation is going well.

chapters.title: Little by Little
chapters.description: Using Suzuki Roshi’s fog-and-shower metaphor, he describes practice as gradual, often unnoticed change.

chapters.title: Conscience and Responsibility
chapters.description: A quoted passage from Václav Havel shifts the talk toward humility, civic responsibility, and respect for life.

chapters.title: Compassion Needs Action
chapters.description: He uses a Bhikkhu Bodhi story to argue that compassion must become concrete conduct, not only intention.

chapters.title: Insight Must Be Lived
chapters.description: Matthew develops the idea that insight is only the beginning and asks how wisdom can be consolidated in daily life.

chapters.title: Practice in Relationship
chapters.description: Through examples from Sylvia Boorstein, Stephen Levine, and a personal eye-contact story, he shows how interpersonal life reveals self-view and tests practice.

chapters.title: Compassion and Giving
chapters.description: He turns to Shantideva and Peter Unger to explore radical compassion, ethical obligation, and what generosity means in a wealthy society.

chapters.title: Doing the Most Good
chapters.description: A long reference to Will MacAskill and effective altruism frames the question of how to use a life well without drifting into complacency.

chapters.title: Without Expecting Applause
chapters.description: The talk closes with Norman Fischer’s lojong slogan on practicing without praise, followed by a brief period of silence.

title: Guided Meditation: Turbulence and Peace (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver begins with a personal story about crying in a dark breathing group, using it to explore how grief, laughter, and a deep peace can coexist. He then moves into a practical account of mental noting, clinging, and the way practice reveals both the movement into selfing and the possibility of letting go. In the second half, he responds to questions about retreat energy, secular mindfulness, ethics, renunciation, and free will, ending with a quiet preference to keep trying without overclaiming certainty.
short_summary: A guided talk on grief, release, and the deeper contours of mindfulness, ethics, and agency.

chapters.title: Grief and Peace
chapters.description: A story from a breathing group opens the talk and shows grief, laughter, and peace arising together.

chapters.title: Noting Experience
chapters.description: He explains mental noting as a way of including more and more of life without standing apart from it.

chapters.title: Craving and Selfing
chapters.description: A compact teaching on how dislike, craving, and clinging crystallize into the felt corner of self.

chapters.title: Letting Go
chapters.description: He repeats the line that clinging never feels as good as letting go and advises not to add more clinging.

chapters.title: Retreat Energy
chapters.description: Matthew reflects on the energy released by intensive practice and how to meet transition without trying to return to an earlier state.

chapters.title: Mindfulness and Renunciation
chapters.description: In response to questions, he distinguishes ethical grounding from the missing piece of renunciation in secular mindfulness.

chapters.title: Agency and Trying
chapters.description: He takes up free will, not-self, and emptiness, then settles on the practical stance of continuing to try.

title: Brian Lesage, Matthew Brensilver: Dharma Debate: Samvega versus Softening and Receiving (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This talk explores a tension at the heart of Dharma practice: the pull of urgency, renunciation, and effort on one side, and softening, receiving, and ease on the other. Matthew Brensilver frames the question through the Buddha’s image of crossing the flood without pushing forward or staying in place, then turns to samvega, the backward step, and the ordinary textures of life as places where practice actually unfolds. The talk moves between teaching, personal story, and a closing poem to suggest that freedom is not somewhere else, but found in meeting experience without grasping it or hardening against it.
short_summary: A reflective Dharma talk on balancing urgency and softness in practice, framed by the Buddha’s middle way and closed with a poem about ordinary life.

chapters.title: The Middle Path
chapters.description: Matthew opens by setting up the Buddha’s middle way and the idea that practice also means finding a wise relationship between two worthy poles.

chapters.title: Urgency and Ease
chapters.description: He develops the contrast between samvega, compassion, and self-love, then introduces the talk as an exploration of effort versus relaxation rather than a fixed formula.

chapters.title: Crossing the Flood
chapters.description: The Ogha Tarana Sutta becomes the central image: crossing without pushing forward or staying in place, which holds the whole talk in view.

chapters.title: Samvega and Dread
chapters.description: Drawing on early discourses and Thanissaro Bhikkhu’s framing, he describes existential shock, fear, and the thorn in the heart as forces that can wake practice up.

chapters.title: Burnout and Softening
chapters.description: Brian Lesage speaks from his own experience of intense Zen training and a painful self-improvement loop, leading into the need for softness, loving-kindness, and receiving experience.

chapters.title: The Backward Step
chapters.description: Using Dogen’s instructions, Matthew names the backward step as simple, immediate awareness—hearing, feeling, and noticing without grabbing or overworking the moment.

chapters.title: Curiosity and Renunciation
chapters.description: The tone turns practical and candid as the speakers reflect on curiosity, the force of craving and aversion, and the need to let go of what looks like honey but does not free the heart.

chapters.title: Freedom in Ordinary Life
chapters.description: Matthew brings the talk down to everyday life, arguing that awakening is not elsewhere but in ordinary human existence as it is actually lived.

chapters.title: What the Living Do
chapters.description: A long reading from Marie Howe’s poem closes the talk with clogged sinks, winter light, spilled coffee, and cherishing the ordinary as a lived expression of practice.

title: Guided Meditation: Clinging and Space (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: This guided meditation begins with settling the body, scanning for how the retreat moment feels, and reassuring the mind that the conditions for freedom are already here. It then turns toward attention, letting go of what is not ours, and meeting craving, aversion, and imperfection with steadiness rather than resistance. In the later sections, Matthew opens out into compassion and nonviolence, drawing on Buddha teachings, Martin Luther King Jr., Shinzen Young, and the Dalai Lama to show how softening the heart can change how we meet experience and one another.
short_summary: A guided retreat meditation on settling, non-clinging, and meeting craving and aversion with steadiness and kindness.

chapters.title: Settling In
chapters.description: The meditation begins by relaxing into the breath, scanning the body and mind, and orienting to the retreat container.

chapters.title: Finding the Anchor
chapters.description: Matthew invites attention to stabilize on the breath and rest in one place instead of flitting across the mind's screen.

chapters.title: Letting Go
chapters.description: He emphasizes that even one breath of presence matters and links wakefulness with a radical inner nonviolence.

chapters.title: Craving and Aversion
chapters.description: A Buddha teaching frames the problem as not pleasure or pain themselves, but craving and aversion, which complicate experience.

chapters.title: More Space Than Expected
chapters.description: The talk opens into the sense that the mind has more room than it assumes, and that guards around the senses can stand down.

chapters.title: Body and Thought
chapters.description: Matthew traces how ruminative thought is powered by bodily activation and how peace with the body changes the mind's story.

chapters.title: Nonviolence and Love
chapters.description: He distinguishes acceptance from passivity, then turns to hatred, Martin Luther King Jr., and the need to meet our own experience with love.

chapters.title: Silence and Integration
chapters.description: The closing moves through Shinzen Young and the Dalai Lama, then returns to retreat practice, shared silence, and noticing where the mind defaults.

title: Samadhi (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver explores samadhi as a natural settling of the mind rather than something forced by will. He moves from the burdens of fragmented attention and habitual storytelling into the ways concentration supports mindfulness, insight, and a quieter relation to experience. The talk closes with practical encouragement: patience, non-striving, and allowing the mind to gather on its own terms.
short_summary: A quiet talk on samadhi as the natural fruit of letting go, attention, and trust in practice.

chapters.title: Opening and presence
chapters.description: Matthew opens lightly, then settles into the sense of practicing together as a field of trust and attention.

chapters.title: Compassion and stability
chapters.description: He uses Brian’s bodhicitta talk as a bridge to show how an open heart depends on an already steady mind.

chapters.title: What samadhi means
chapters.description: He reframes enlightenment and concentration as less friction with experience, and as peace rather than a project of control.

chapters.title: Attention under pressure
chapters.description: A critique of technological distraction follows, including Tristan Harris and a personal reflection on phones and attention.

chapters.title: Shelter from the storm
chapters.description: He describes how samadhi narrows the field of awareness, softens bombardment, and supports insight into body and mind.

chapters.title: Thought and story
chapters.description: The talk turns to the habit of narrating experience, the fear of silence, and the need to meet thought without shame.

chapters.title: Pleasure and attachment
chapters.description: Using a Buddha sutta and the lake simile, he describes the joy of concentration while warning against making bliss the goal.

chapters.title: Patience and letting go
chapters.description: He emphasizes patience, dependence on causes and conditions, and the loosening of self-protective habits as the mind gathers.

chapters.title: Natural stillness
chapters.description: An Ajahn Brahm image of the leaf leads into a closing reminder that stillness cannot be engineered, followed by a short sit and thanks.

title: Metta Reflections: Opening to Life Requires Love (Retreat at Spirit Rock)
speaker: Matthew Brensilver
podcast_description: Matthew Brensilver frames metta as more than a feeling: it can cultivate love, steady concentration, or bring buried aversion to the surface for purification. He then widens the teaching by showing how insight practice and loving-kindness interweave, using a teen retreat story to illustrate how simple care can reach resistance more directly than force. The talk closes in a quiet metta instruction, emphasizing humility, the vulnerability of being human, and the ordinary worthiness of care.
short_summary: A grounded reflection on metta as cultivation, concentration, and purification, ending in a gentle guided loving-kindness practice.

chapters.title: What Metta Does
chapters.description: Brensilver introduces love as a central human good and explains metta as a practice that can work in more than one way.

chapters.title: Three Functions
chapters.description: He lays out metta as cultivation, concentration, and purification, including the surprise of aversion arising in practice.

chapters.title: Love and Insight
chapters.description: The talk widens into the claim that vipassana needs a soft heart, and that insight and kindness become increasingly interwoven.

chapters.title: Teen Retreat Story
chapters.description: Brensilver tells a story from teen retreats, using Michelle MacDonald’s example to show how simple touch and care can open a defended heart.

chapters.title: Connection and Poignancy
chapters.description: He reflects on silence, struggle, and connection as movements toward the poignancy of life and a love that overflows into others.

chapters.title: Facing Innocence
chapters.description: The teaching turns toward the innocence of wanting happiness, the wish to forgive ourselves, and the way self-harshness resists the unpredictability of life.

chapters.title: Softening Through Uncertainty
chapters.description: He names the ungovernability of life and suggests that insight into uncertainty can soften rather than harden the heart.

chapters.title: Guided Metta
chapters.description: Brensilver leads a gentle metta practice, inviting the listener to repeat the phrases and listen for their reverberation in the body.

chapters.title: Humility and Care
